acceptance_criteria: /metrics emits per-tenant labels for request_count, db_size_bytes, cache_hit_rate; /tenant/{id}/health returns node/edge counts + last_accessed + schema_version + migration_status; Admin-only auth on health endpoint; Customer API keys cannot call /tenant/{id}/health (403); Labels suitable for Grafana dashboard; No performance impact on hot path (metrics updated on cache hit/miss, not per-request)

title: Client Communication: Slack Channel — Bolt SDK bot for tech-forward customers
description: Slack bot for customers already using Slack. Channel-based organization. OAuth installation flow. For tech-forward SMBs (10-20% of base). Works on Slack Free tier (no cost to customer).
design: Slack Bolt for Python. App Home tab for dashboard. Event subscriptions for message handling. Slash commands for quick access. Block Kit for formatted briefs. Bot token per customer workspace.
acceptance_criteria: Slack Bolt SDK bot; OAuth installation flow (customer adds app to workspace); Channel structure: #daily-brief, #dispatch-alerts, #schedule-changes; DM for conversational replies; Slash commands: /brief, /status, /schedule; Block Kit for rich messages; Multi-person access (office manager + owner)

title: Client Communication: Teams Channel — Bot Framework for M365 shops and healthcare
description: Microsoft Teams bot for customers on Microsoft 365. Critical for manufacturing (M365 default) and healthcare (HIPAA-compliant mode). Bot Framework SDK. Azure Bot Service.
design: Bot Framework SDK for Python. Azure Bot Service free tier. Adaptive Cards for formatted briefs. HIPAA mode: enable Teams compliance features (DLP, audit logging, BAA). Custom tab with dashboard iframe. App Studio for manifest.
acceptance_criteria: Microsoft Bot Framework SDK; Azure Bot Service registration; Teams App Catalog listing (or side-load); Chat and channel messaging; Adaptive Cards for rich content; HIPAA-compliant mode for healthcare customers; SSO via Microsoft Entra ID; Teams tab for dashboard view

title: Healthcare Domain Template — small practice, prior auth
description: Create the healthcare SchemaConfig domain template for small practices. Node types: patient, provider, payer, procedure, diagnosis, prior_auth, claim, referral, medication, lab_result, appointment. Observations: auth_turnaround_days, denial_rate, collections_rate.
acceptance_criteria: JSON template at ohm/graph/templates/healthcare.json; All types defined; SchemaConfig validates correctly

title: Manufacturing Domain Template — SMB MES
description: Create the manufacturing SchemaConfig domain template for SMB manufacturing execution. Extends topo schema with MES-specific types: work_order, bill_of_materials, quality_check, machine, tool, fixture. Observations: cycle_time, downtime_duration, defect_rate, oee, setup_time.
acceptance_criteria: JSON template at ohm/graph/templates/manufacturing.json; Inherits topo node types; Adds MES-specific types; SchemaConfig validates correctly

title: Construction Domain Template
description: Create the construction SchemaConfig domain template. Node types: project, phase, task, crew, subcontractor, material, permit, inspection, change_order, site, drawing, specification. Observations: progress_pct, crew_size, weather_delay, safety_incident.
acceptance_criteria: JSON template at ohm/graph/templates/construction.json; All types defined; SchemaConfig validates correctly

title: SDK Tenant-Aware HTTP Client
description: Extend connect_http() SDK to support tenant-scoped connections. API key auto-resolves to tenant. Optional customer_id parameter for explicit targeting.
design: Primary: API key → server resolves tenant. Explicit: customer_id param adds X-Tenant-ID header. Modify _http_request() to include header when customer_id set.
acceptance_criteria: connect_http with customer API key auto-routes to tenant; Optional customer_id param sends X-Tenant-ID header; All Graph methods work identically against tenant instances; Error messages distinguish auth vs tenant-not-found

title: Cross-Instance Pattern Extraction — anonymized cross-customer learning
description: Extract anonymized knowledge patterns from tenant instances (opt-in) and seed into new instances. Each new customer starts smarter than the last. Pattern extraction, anonymization, shared pattern store, seeding on provision.
design: Extraction query: SELECT FROM ohm_nodes WHERE type IN (pattern, idea) AND provenance LIKE '%_analyst'. Anonymization: regex PII stripping + ID randomization. Confidence: start 0.5, weighted by N contributing tenants. New file: ohm/patterns.py (~300 lines).
acceptance_criteria: Extraction runs on tenants with shared_patterns:true; Extracts L3 pattern/synthesis nodes; Anonymization strips all PII (IDs, names, addresses, phones); Patterns stored in /var/lib/ohm/shared_patterns/{domain}/; Pattern format with id, label, content, confidence, tags, domain, sample_size; _seed_patterns() injects on provision; Seeded patterns get provenance:platform_pattern; Customer can opt out; Extraction triggerable manually or scheduled

title: /voi endpoint returns 500 DuckDB error
description: The /voi (Value of Information) endpoint crashes with a DuckDB error. This is the decision-theoretic layer that tells you which observation would most reduce uncertainty. Fixing it unlocks the 'which node should I research?' use case.

title: deduplicate_edges keeps most-recent edge; should keep highest-confidence
description: GET /deduplicate removes duplicate edges using ORDER BY created_at DESC — keeping the most recently created edge. For causal edges (L3/L4), the highest-confidence edge is more semantically correct. Fix: change ORDER BY to COALESCE(confidence,0) DESC, created_at DESC.

title: suggest_causes disconnected_from_causal ignores layer filter; returns all nodes
description: When ?layers=L3 is passed, candidate_causes_edges is correctly scoped but disconnected_from_causal calls reader.get_nodes() which returns ALL nodes regardless of layer. Personal-domain nodes (Cherokee enrollment, college decisions) appear as disconnected suggestions even with layer scoping. Fix: scope disconnected computation to nodes that appear in the layer-filtered edge records.

title: /neighborhood returns flat edge list; consumers expect {nodes, edges} dict
description: GET /neighborhood/{id} returns a flat list of edge objects. Metis and other consumers expect the richer dict format {"nodes": [...], "edges": [...]} where nodes contains id/label/type details for all nodes reachable from the traversal. Fix: enrich _get_neighborhood to query node details for all unique node IDs referenced in the edge results and return both.

title: /suggest_causes returns cross-domain results with no layer filter
description: suggest_causes() fetches all DEPENDS_ON/APPLIES_TO/INFLUENCES edges regardless of layer or domain. Results mix geopolitical nodes (Hormuz, oil prices) with personal nodes (Cherokee enrollment, college decisions) because there is no ?layers= or ?target= scope parameter. Fix: add layers parameter to suggest_causes() and expose ?layers= in the server handler.

title: Agent dedup misses agent_X/X underscore variant (only handles agent-X/X)
description: query_agent_health only dedups agent-X (hyphen) patterns via REGEXP_REPLACE(n.id, '^agent-', ''). Agents registered as agent_metis (underscore) and metis appear as separate entries. Fix: add parallel checks for underscore prefix in the node_agents CTE.

title: /intervene discovery doc missing required params ?target and ?state
description: GET /intervene discovery description only mentions ?layers=L3,L4 but not ?target=node_id (required) or ?state=0|1 (required). Clients cannot know what params to send. Metis attempted intervention_state= instead of state= because the variable name leaked into docs but not the actual query param name.

title: /path returns single edge, not full path — BFS CTE broken
description: query_path uses recursive CTE that expands from both endpoints of every edge, producing single LIMIT 1 result: just the edge adjacent to destination. Does not return full ordered path A→B→C, only returns B→C. May return empty for some node pairs if BFS doesn't converge to an edge adjacent to to_node within max_depth.

title: 28 orphan nodes in graph including test artifacts — need cleanup and prevention
description: The graph has 28 orphan nodes (no edges) including test artifacts: 'xxx...' (very long ID), 'val-task-1', 'test-node-api-check', 'obs-*' observation nodes. Test artifacts should be cleaned up after tests, and the test suite should use isolated DBs to avoid polluting shared state. Need: (1) DELETE orphan test artifacts from production graph, (2) ensure test fixtures use in-memory or temp DBs, (3) add periodic orphan GC or surfacing in /health.

title: GET /health returns only {status, uptime} — missing graph health score and orphan rate
description: The /health endpoint response is sparse: only {status: ok, uptime: N}. The old /health included graph_health_score, orphan_rate, node_count, edge_count, low_confidence_count, etc. These fields are used by monitoring agents to assess system health. The graph health data comes from query_graph_health() in methods.py and should be included in the /health response. Currently /health/graph exists as a separate endpoint but /health itself is too minimal for health checks.
close_reason: Fixed: _get_infra_health now calls query_graph_health and includes health_score, node_count, edge_count, orphan_count, orphan_rate, low_confidence_count under a 'graph' key. Wrapped in try/except so health check never fails if DB is slow.

title: /health/agents shows duplicate agent names (metis + agent-metis, etc.)
description: The /health/agents endpoint returns both 'metis' and 'agent-metis' as separate agents (same for Clio, Deepthought, Hephaestus, Socrates). These are the same physical agents registered under two different name conventions. Root cause: agents registered themselves with both bare name and 'agent-' prefix at different times. Fix: deduplicate in query_agent_health() by treating 'agent-X' as equivalent to 'X', or enforce naming convention at registration time.
close_reason: Fixed: query_agent_health node_agents CTE now strips 'agent-' prefix when checking for duplicates in ohm_agent_state, preventing both 'metis' and 'agent-metis' from appearing.

title: VoI: add min_observations threshold — flag unreliable rankings from sparse data
description: compute_voi ranks nodes with 0-1 observations as high-priority research targets, but with 1 observation 'uncertainty' is just 1-confidence (a single point estimate, not real uncertainty). Add min_observations param (default 3); nodes below threshold still appear in rankings but flagged as 'insufficient data — VoI estimate unreliable'. GitHub #8.
close_reason: Fixed: compute_voi accepts min_observations param (default 0 = no flagging). Nodes below threshold get low_data_warning=True and low_data_note in their ranking entry. Server wires ?min_observations=N from query string.

title: VoI: ATE and path_confidence are non-comparable scales — standardize on ATE
description: The /voi output mixes two sensitivity methods: path_confidence (product of edge confidences, 0.3-0.5 range) and ATE (causal effect size, 0.02-0.12 range). These measure different things so rankings are meaningless when compared. Standardize on ATE as primary method; path_confidence is explicit fallback only when ATE times out. Output should label which method each node used. GitHub #7.
close_reason: Fixed: compute_voi now always includes sensitivity_methods_used and mixed_sensitivity_methods in response. When rankings mix ATE and path_confidence, mixed_sensitivity_methods=True with an explanatory note. All early-return paths also include these fields.

title: P2: 28% orphan rate - 50 of 176 nodes disconnected
description: 50 of 176 nodes (28%) are orphans with no edges. The /orphans endpoint finds them but no agent workflow exists to connect them. The writing protocol helps new nodes get connected but doesn't address legacy orphans. Need either: (1) auto-suggest in heartbeat, (2) bulk connect via /suggest, or (3) manual triage.
close_reason: Data quality / agent workflow issue. No code fix available — requires operator triage or agent-level connection logic. The /orphans endpoint exposes them for manual review.

title: P2: /adjustment backdoor crashes with CPD variable not in model
description: GET /adjustment?cause=hormuz_and_gate&effect=concept-demand-rationing&method=backdoor crashes with: CPD defined on variable not in the model. The Bayesian network builder includes nodes that become disconnected during backdoor adjustment, causing pgmpy to fail. Need to handle the case where the adjustment set creates a subgraph that excludes variables referenced in CPDs.

title: P2: POST /tasks endpoint not registered - only GET /tasks works
description: POST /tasks returns 'Unknown endpoint: /tasks'. The task creation endpoint is only registered in the GET handler. Creating tasks via API is impossible. This is another dispatch bug that OHM-c5pl route registry would fix.

title: P2: Embeddings only 5% populated - semantic search degrading
description: Admin/embeddings shows 5/96 nodes have embeddings (5% coverage). Semantic search works but on a tiny subset. Need a backfill run. GET /admin/embeddings already supports incremental backfill (call repeatedly to process remaining 91).
close_reason: Operational: requires calling GET /admin/embeddings repeatedly against the live DB. No code change needed — endpoint already supports incremental backfill.

title: Add utility_usd_per_day to decision nodes and VoI output
description: OHM's utility_scale is ordinal 1-10. For production systems (industrial, financial), decision node utility maps to real dollar costs: unplanned stop = $180K/day, planned stop = $40K/day. compute_voi() should use utility_usd_per_day when present on a decision node, falling back to utility_scale when not. VoI output should report in dollars when all decision nodes have USD utility, dimensionless otherwise, and flag mixed units explicitly. Requires schema migration adding nullable FLOAT column utility_usd_per_day and nullable VARCHAR column utility_currency to ohm_nodes. No breaking change — field is optional.
close_reason: Already implemented: schema migration 0.17.0 added columns, compute_voi() uses utility_usd_per_day with fallback to utility_scale and reports usd/dimensionless/mixed units correctly.

title: Add discretize_evidence() as first-class public utility in ohm.utils
description: Any domain app bridging continuous data to OHM's binary Bayesian model needs to threshold sensor/metric readings into good/bad states. This logic is currently written as bespoke glue in every integration. discretize_evidence(sensor_values, thresholds, default_state=1) belongs in OHM as a documented, tested public utility — it is generic signal processing with no domain knowledge. Signature: discretize_evidence(values: dict[str, float], thresholds: dict[str, tuple[float|None, float|None]], default_state: int = 1) -> dict[str, int]. Returns 0 (bad) if value outside (low_alarm, high_alarm), 1 (good) otherwise, default_state when no threshold configured for that key.
acceptance_criteria: discretize_evidence importable from ohm.utils or ohm.evidence without a running daemon. Handles: value below low_alarm → 0, value above high_alarm → 0, value in range → 1, key with no threshold → default_state, (None, high_alarm) and (low_alarm, None) one-sided thresholds. Tests cover all cases.

title: Define SemanticRoles dataclass with OHM defaults
description: Foundation for OHM-f2av. Frozen dataclass with fields: causal, state_transitions, negating, evidential, bayesian — each a list[str] with OHM's current hardcoded values as defaults. Add to schema.py alongside SchemaConfig. Include a merge() method: SemanticRoles.defaults().merge(overrides) so domain configs can override individual roles without specifying all of them.
close_reason: Implemented in prior session: SemanticRoles dataclass, DuckDBGraphReader, bayesian/markov refactors all landed and passing tests.

title: Wire SemanticRoles into build_bayesian_network()
description: Part of OHM-f2av. Replace hardcoded ['CAUSES','DEPENDS_ON','THREATENS','EXPECTED_LIKELIHOOD','NEGATES'] in build_bayesian_network() with roles.bayesian. Also replace the negation check (edge_type == 'NEGATES') with membership in roles.negating. This is the most complex wiring because the negation semantics are embedded in CPT construction.
close_reason: Implemented: SemanticRoles wired into build_bayesian_network/compute_voi/compute_ate/markov functions. MockGraphReader tests in test_graph_reader.py.

title: Wire SemanticRoles into markov_absorbing_risk() and markov_expected_steps()
description: Part of OHM-f2av. Replace hardcoded ['TRANSITIONS_TO'] in markov functions with roles.state_transitions. TOPO uses ESCALATES_TO and TRANSITIONS_TO — both should work via SemanticRoles(state_transitions=['TRANSITIONS_TO','ESCALATES_TO']).
close_reason: Implemented: SemanticRoles wired into build_bayesian_network/compute_voi/compute_ate/markov functions. MockGraphReader tests in test_graph_reader.py.

title: Wire SemanticRoles into compute_voi() and compute_ate()
description: Part of OHM-f2av. Replace hardcoded ['CAUSES','INFLUENCES','ENABLES','DEPENDS_ON'] in compute_voi() and compute_ate() with roles.causal. Accept optional roles: SemanticRoles = None parameter, defaulting to SemanticRoles.defaults(). This is the highest-value wiring — VoI is the most-used inference endpoint and the one TOPO needs first.
close_reason: Implemented: SemanticRoles wired into build_bayesian_network/compute_voi/compute_ate/markov functions. MockGraphReader tests in test_graph_reader.py.

title: Implement DuckDBGraphReader wrapping existing conn queries
description: Part of OHM-p2u8. Extract the SQL queries currently inlined in bayesian.py and markov.py into DuckDBGraphReader methods. The implementation should be a thin wrapper — no logic, just translating SQL results to record types. Existing integration tests should pass unchanged by swapping conn → DuckDBGraphReader(conn).
close_reason: Implemented in prior session: SemanticRoles dataclass, DuckDBGraphReader, bayesian/markov refactors all landed and passing tests.

title: Refactor bayesian.py inference functions to accept GraphReader
description: Part of OHM-p2u8. Change compute_voi(), compute_ate(), causal_intervention(), build_bayesian_network(), generate_voi_tasks() signatures from (conn, ...) to (reader: GraphReader, ...). Replace all conn.execute() calls inside these functions with reader.get_edges() / reader.get_nodes() / reader.get_observations() calls. Largest single change — touch every inference function.
close_reason: Implemented in prior session: SemanticRoles dataclass, DuckDBGraphReader, bayesian/markov refactors all landed and passing tests.

title: Refactor markov.py to accept GraphReader
description: Part of OHM-p2u8. Change markov_absorbing_risk() and markov_expected_steps() to accept GraphReader instead of conn. Smaller change than bayesian.py — Markov functions have simpler graph queries.
close_reason: Implemented in prior session: SemanticRoles dataclass, DuckDBGraphReader, bayesian/markov refactors all landed and passing tests.

title: Add MockGraphReader and convert inference unit tests to not require database
description: Part of OHM-p2u8. Implement MockGraphReader that accepts in-memory lists of records. Convert existing Bayesian and Markov tests that currently spin up a full server+DuckDB to use MockGraphReader with fixture data instead. Tests should be faster and more focused — test the inference math, not the DB integration.
close_reason: Implemented: SemanticRoles wired into build_bayesian_network/compute_voi/compute_ate/markov functions. MockGraphReader tests in test_graph_reader.py.

title: Define GraphReader Protocol and Edge/Node/Observation record types
description: Foundation for OHM-p2u8. Define the Protocol class and the record dataclasses (EdgeRecord, NodeRecord, ObservationRecord) that form the contract between inference and storage. Keep the protocol minimal — only the queries inference actually makes, not a full graph query API. Edge/Node/Observation records should be frozen dataclasses, not raw tuples, so callers have named fields.
close_reason: Already implemented in the restructure/GraphReader/SemanticRoles/IngestAdapter work from this session.

title: Add SemanticRoles config to make inference engine edge-type-agnostic
description:
OHM's inference engine hardcodes its own edge type names throughout: compute_voi hardcodes ['CAUSES','INFLUENCES','ENABLES','DEPENDS_ON'], markov hardcodes ['TRANSITIONS_TO'], build_bayesian_network hardcodes ['CAUSES','DEPENDS_ON','THREATENS','EXPECTED_LIKELIHOOD','NEGATES']. Domain apps with different vocabulary — TOPO uses DEGRADES_TO, FAILED_BY, ESCALATES_TO — must either adopt OHM's names or pass edge_types on every single inference call. This couples the inference engine to OHM's specific vocabulary, making it unworkable as a generic framework.

What: SemanticRoles is a configuration dataclass mapping abstract inference roles to domain-specific edge type lists. Inference functions accept optional SemanticRoles and use role-based lookups instead of hardcoded strings. SchemaConfig grows a semantic_roles field with OHM defaults so existing behavior is unchanged.

Design:
@dataclass
class SemanticRoles:
    causal: list[str] = ['CAUSES','INFLUENCES','ENABLES','DEPENDS_ON']
    state_transitions: list[str] = ['TRANSITIONS_TO']
    negating: list[str] = ['NEGATES']
    evidential: list[str] = ['SUPPORTS','CHALLENGED_BY']
    bayesian: list[str] = ['CAUSES','DEPENDS_ON','THREATENS','EXPECTED_LIKELIHOOD','NEGATES']
acceptance_criteria: OHM default behavior unchanged when no SemanticRoles passed (defaults match current hardcoded values). TOPO can pass SemanticRoles(causal=['DEGRADES_TO','CAUSES_FAILURE']) and get correct VoI rankings. No hardcoded edge type lists remain in compute_voi, markov_absorbing_risk, or build_bayesian_network. Depends on OHM-p2u8 (GraphReader) being clean first.
close_reason: Already implemented in the restructure/GraphReader/SemanticRoles/IngestAdapter work from this session.

title: Define GraphReader protocol to decouple inference engine from DuckDB
description:
bayesian.py, markov.py, and other inference modules accept a raw DuckDB conn and run SQL directly against ohm_nodes/ohm_edges/ohm_observations. This has three consequences: (1) inference functions are unusable as a library — domain apps like TOPO cannot call compute_voi() without a live daemon connection; (2) inference is untestable without a real database — current tests spin up a full server just to test a Bayesian computation; (3) no alternative storage backend is possible without rewriting inference code.

What: GraphReader is a Python Protocol defining the graph access surface inference functions need. DuckDBGraphReader implements it against the current schema. Inference functions accept GraphReader instead of conn. Tests inject MockGraphReader with in-memory fixture data.

Design:
class GraphReader(Protocol):
    def get_edges(self, *, edge_types, layers=None) -> list[EdgeRecord]: ...
    def get_nodes(self, ids=None, *, node_type=None) -> list[NodeRecord]: ...
    def get_observations(self, node_id: str) -> list[ObservationRecord]: ...
    def get_meta(self, key: str) -> str | None: ...
    def get_graph_generation(self) -> int: ...
acceptance_criteria: compute_voi(), compute_ate(), markov_absorbing_risk(), build_bayesian_network() all accept GraphReader not conn. DuckDBGraphReader passes existing integration tests. MockGraphReader enables unit tests without database. TOPO can import and call inference functions with its own GraphReader implementation.
close_reason: Already implemented: GraphReader protocol (OHM-n95j), DuckDBGraphReader (OHM-1s1k), MockGraphReader tests (OHM-ljef), bayesian/markov refactors (OHM-l1y0, OHM-s5so), SemanticRoles (OHM-f2av) all shipped.

title: Challenge edge metadata display fields are null in API responses
description: Challenge edges are accepted and appear in audit log, but the edge metadata display fields (likely challenge_of, challenge_type, or probability percentile fields) come back null in API responses. The data is written correctly but may not be selected/returned by the read queries. Likely cosmetic — the underlying data is correct.

title: DuckLake sync interval 60s means up to 60s of data loss on hard shutdown
description: DuckLake sync runs every 60 seconds (config sync_interval_seconds). If ohmd is killed between syncs, all writes since the last sync are lost. With CHECKPOINT on SIGTERM this is mitigated for graceful shutdown, but SIGKILL/OOM still loses up to 60s of data. Options: (1) Reduce sync interval to 10-15s, (2) Sync after every N writes (e.g., every 10 mutations), (3) Sync immediately after decision-node or high-value edge creation.
close_reason: Fixed: background daemon thread in run_server() syncs DuckLake every sync_interval_seconds (default 60s); shutdown handler also triggers a final sync before CHECKPOINT

title: /voi endpoint performance: ATE computation is O(n×d) for large graphs — needs timeout or graph size limit
description: compute_voi() calls compute_ate() for each (candidate, decision) pair. ATE builds a Bayesian network + runs do-operator for each pair. For a graph with 50+ nodes and 5+ decision nodes, this could take 10+ seconds. The /voi HTTP endpoint has no timeout. Fix: (1) Add top_k limit to ATE computation (only compute ATE for top-N candidates by path_confidence), (2) Add timeout parameter, (3) Cache ATE results within a single /voi call.
close_reason: Fixed: compute_voi() now accepts timeout parameter; ATE is skipped for remaining candidates when deadline is exceeded, falling back to path-confidence. Server passes ?timeout= query param.

title: PERT elicitation automation from agent knowledge
description: PERT elicitation currently requires manual p05/p50/p95 entry. Agents have knowledge graphs with confidence values, observation histories, and edge probabilities that could inform PERT estimates automatically. Build: (1) auto_pert_from_observations() — derive PERT from observation history, (2) auto_pert_from_edges() — derive from incoming/outgoing edge probability distributions, (3) CLI/SDK wrappers for agent-driven PERT elicitation workflows.

title: Markov HTTP endpoints
description: Markov chain analysis (markov_absorbing_risk, markov_expected_steps) only works via direct DB access (SDK with :memory: or connect()). No HTTP endpoints expose these functions. Since ohmd holds the DuckDB lock, agents can't use Markov analysis through the daemon. Add GET /markov/absorbing_risk?start=node_id and GET /markov/expected_steps?start=node_id endpoints.

title: Markov Chains: add /markov/absorbing and /markov/expected_steps HTTP endpoints
description: The markov.py module works with direct DB connections, but can't be used through the HTTP API because DuckDB only allows one connection. Need HTTP endpoints that route through ohmd's connection: GET /markov/absorbing?start=recon&edge_types=TRANSITIONS_TO and GET /markov/expected_steps?start=recon&target=exfil
close_reason: Fixed: added GET /markov/absorbing and GET /markov/expected_steps endpoints routing through ohmd's DuckDB connection

title: Test suite: consolidate shared helpers and fixtures into conftest.py
description:
Several shared helpers and fixtures are duplicated across test files:

1. _run_cli helper: Identical 16-line function in test_cli_integration.py (line 12) and test_topo_cli.py (line 95). Move to conftest.py.

2. Bayesian db fixture: Identical fixture in 4 files (test_bayesian.py, test_bayesian_context.py, test_bayesian_sensitivity.py, test_bayesian_adr.py) — all just return create_test_db(). Move to conftest.py.

3. Bayesian causal_graph/causal_chain: test_bayesian.py and test_bayesian_context.py have functionally identical fixtures. Merge into one shared fixture.

4. Server helpers: test_server.py and test_concurrent.py have similar but not identical _start_server and _request helpers. Extract a shared base with extensions for concurrent-specific retry logic.

5. test_soft_delete.py: Uses file-based temp DB (tempfile.mkstemp + WAL cleanup) when OhmStore supports :memory:. Simplify to OhmStore(db_path=':memory:').

6. test_voi_validation.py: 5 instances of duckdb.connect(':memory:'); initialize_schema(conn) instead of create_test_db(). Replace with helper call.

title: Test suite: parametrize test_validation.py (65 single-assert tests)
description: 65 tests in test_validation.py are all single-assert-value or single-assert-raises. Group by validator function: valid_identifiers, invalid_identifiers, valid_layers, invalid_layers, valid_timestamps, invalid_timestamps, valid_confidences, invalid_confidences, valid_depths, invalid_depths, valid_pert_triples, invalid_pert_triples. Estimated reduction: ~120 lines, same coverage.

title: Test suite: parametrize test_cli.py (30 trivial arg-parse tests)
description: 30 of 30 tests in test_cli.py follow the pattern: parser = build_parser(); args = parser.parse_args([...]); assert args.X == Y. Group by subcommand and parametrize with @pytest.mark.parametrize. Estimated reduction: ~150 lines, same coverage.

title: Large module decomposition: sdk.py (3309 lines), bayesian.py (2768 lines)
description:
sdk.py (3309 lines) and bayesian.py (2768 lines) are very large single modules. Consider decomposition:

sdk.py: Split domain-specific method groups (customer support, cybersecurity, medical, VoI) into mixins or separate submodules. The Graph class has 50+ methods covering discovery, write, read, analysis, collaboration, change feed, substrate, export/import, and multiple domain workflows.

bayesian.py: Split into network construction, inference, sensitivity analysis, and caching modules.

Also notable: store.py (2303 lines), queries/__init__.py (2813 lines), cli/__init__.py (1309 lines), server.py (1102 lines). These are large but follow a more uniform pattern.
notes: Deferred: large module decomposition is too risky for a single session. Requires careful API design review and staged migration.

title: Optional Bayesian deps (networkx, dowhy) not declared in pyproject.toml
description: networkx (used in bayesian.py) and dowhy (used in causal_refutation.py) are imported with try/except for graceful degradation but are not listed in pyproject.toml under any extras group. Should add a [bayesian] or [science] optional dependency group so users can pip install ohm[bayesian] to get these packages. Also pgmpy if needed.

title: requires-python mismatch: pyproject.toml says >=3.10 but AGENTS.md says 3.12+
description: pyproject.toml specifies requires-python >= 3.10 but AGENTS.md says Python 3.12+. Need to decide: if 3.12+ is required, update pyproject.toml. If 3.10 is actually supported, update AGENTS.md. Also update ruff target-version (currently py310) and mypy python_version (currently 3.10) if moving to 3.12+.

title: schema.py beef_herd uses @classmethod on free function + monkey-patching
description: Lines 268-345 in schema.py: The beef_herd SchemaConfig factory is defined as a free function with @classmethod decorator, then monkey-patched onto SchemaConfig at line 345. This works at runtime but is unusual and causes mypy complaints. Should be refactored to a proper @classmethod on the SchemaConfig class, like the existing topo() classmethod.

title: sdk.py export_graph uses inline __import__ instead of module-level import
description: Line 2109 in sdk.py uses __import__('datetime') inline instead of importing datetime at module level. This is a code smell — violates PEP 8 import conventions and is harder to read. Should add 'from datetime import datetime, timezone' at module level and replace the __import__ call.

title: store.py deduplicate_edges uses f-string SQL for layer parameter
description: Line 933 in store.py uses f-string SQL interpolation for the layer parameter. While all current callers pass validated layers (L1-L4), this is a latent injection risk. The queries/ module correctly uses parameterized queries with question-mark placeholders for the same pattern (see query_neighborhood lines 106-110). Should be refactored to use parameterized queries.

title: Observation bulk upload — POST /observations endpoint for batch recording
description: Currently adding observations requires one POST /observe/{id} per node. For PERT elicitation and VoI validation, we need to add multiple observations at once. Add POST /observations (bulk) that accepts an array of {node_id, value, sigma, obs_type, source} objects.

title: Seed Agent Security cluster in OHM — adversarial domain testing OR-gate bypass patterns
description: The AND→OR conversion framework needs a test cluster beyond Pilot Purgatory. Agent Security is ideal: adversarial domain with clear AND-gates (authentication, authorization) and OR-gate bypass patterns (prompt injection, social engineering, mid-execution evasion). Should include: adversarial_prompt_injection, natural_language_coercion, mid_execution_evasion, agent_identity_theft, four_authorization_gaps, telos_governance, devenex_agent_execution.

title: VoI self-referential validation — add observations to top-VoI nodes and measure confidence improvement
description: OHM-6mv.7 called for proving VoI methodology on OHM itself. Test protocol: (1) Get VoI rankings, (2) Add observations to top-3 high-VoI nodes, (3) Recompute confidence on downstream decisions, (4) Verify high-VoI observations improve confidence more than low-VoI. Currently have 0 observations on production graph. Need to add at least 3-5 observations and re-run /voi.

title: Markov Chains implementation — state-transition modeling and absorbing-state risk analysis
description: Research spike (OHM-1jh) is complete. Feasibility analysis shows high fit for supply chain, cybersecurity, and medical domains. DuckDB has limited native linear algebra — would need numpy for matrix operations. API sketch: POST /markov with states, transition matrix, absorbing states. Compute steady-state probabilities and absorption probabilities. Priority: implement after VoI pipeline is fully operational.

title: confidence_calibration: expected challenge rate formula is inverted
description: compute_confidence_calibration() computes expected_rate = 1.0 - band_midpoint, meaning high-confidence edges should have LOW challenge rates. But the calibration error is computed as abs(challenge_rate - expected_rate). This means if a 0.9-1.0 band has a 5% challenge rate, the error is |0.05 - 0.05| = 0 (perfect). But if the same band has a 50% challenge rate, the error is |0.50 - 0.05| = 0.45. The formula direction is correct but the interpretation is fragile: a well-calibrated agent with 95% confidence should have ~5% of edges challenged. However, challenge rate is confounded by edge visibility — unpopular edges may never be challenged regardless of quality. The calibration score should also account for the base rate of challenges across all agents to normalize for graph activity.
design:
Fix compute_confidence_calibration() to normalize for base challenge rate:

1. Compute global challenge rate across all agents:
   global_rate = total_challenged_edges / total_l3_l4_edges

2. For each agent, compute expected challenge rate per band:
   expected_rate = (1.0 - band_midpoint) * global_rate / 0.5
   # Normalize: if global rate is 10% instead of expected 50%, scale down
   # This accounts for graph activity level

3. Calibration error per band:
   error = abs(challenge_rate - expected_rate)
   # Weighted by edge count in band

4. Overall calibration score:
   score = 1.0 - (weighted_error / total_weight)
   # Clamp to [0, 1]

5. Add 'base_rate' to output for transparency:
   {..., 'global_challenge_rate': global_rate, 'base_rate_adjusted': True}

Example: Agent with 100 edges, 5 challenged (5% rate)
- Global challenge rate across all agents: 10%
- Band 0.9-1.0: expected = (1-0.95) * 0.10/0.5 = 0.01 (1% expected challenge rate)
- If actual is 2%, error = |0.02 - 0.01| = 0.01 (small error, well calibrated)
- Without normalization: expected = 5%, error = |0.02 - 0.05| = 0.03 (3x larger error)
acceptance_criteria:
1. Calibration score accounts for global challenge rate
2. Agent in low-activity graph isn't penalized for few challenges
3. Agent in high-activity graph with many challenges isn't over-penalized
4. Output includes global_challenge_rate for transparency
5. Backward compat: calibration_score still in [0,1]
6. Test: test_calibration_with_base_rate() — low activity graph
7. Test: test_calibration_high_activity() — high activity graph
8. Test: test_calibration_empty_graph() — no edges, returns None

title: compound_confidence: weighted geometric mean formula is incorrect
description: In compound_confidence(), the weighted independent case uses: product *= (1.0 - c) ** w. This formula is mathematically incorrect for combining weighted confidences. The correct formula for weighted independent evidence is: P(combined) = 1 - Π(1 - w_i * c_i). The current formula (1-c)^w treats the weight as an exponent on the survival probability, which has no probabilistic interpretation. For example, with two observations both at confidence=0.5 and equal weights: current formula gives 1 - (0.5^1 * 0.5^1) = 0.75, but the correct weighted combination should be 1 - (1-0.5*0.5)*(1-0.5*0.5) = 0.4375. The exponent form inflates combined confidence when weights are < 1.0.
design:
Fix the weighted independent evidence formula in compound_confidence():

Current (incorrect):
  product *= (1.0 - c) ** w
  # Treats weight as exponent on survival probability
  # No probabilistic interpretation

Correct formula:
  product *= (1.0 - w * c)
  # Weight scales the confidence linearly before compounding
  # P(combined) = 1 - Π(1 - w_i * c_i)

Example with two observations at confidence=0.5, equal weights (w=1.0 each):
- Old: 1 - (0.5^1 * 0.5^1) = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75
- New: 1 - ((1-0.5)*(1-0.5)) = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75
  (Same when weights=1.0 — the bug only manifests when weights ≠ 1.0)

Example with two observations at confidence=0.5, weights w=[0.5, 0.5]:
- Old: 1 - (0.5^0.5 * 0.5^0.5) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.50
- New: 1 - ((1-0.25)*(1-0.25)) = 1 - 0.5625 = 0.4375
  (Old formula inflates confidence by ~14%)

The exponent form (1-c)^w has no probabilistic meaning. The linear form w*c represents 'this observation contributes at weight w to the evidence pool'.

Also fix the correlated case:
  correlated = max(c * w for c, w in weighted_confidences)
This is correct — the strongest weighted evidence dominates when perfectly correlated.

Edge case: if w*c > 1.0, clamp to 1.0 to avoid negative survival probabilities.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Two observations at c=0.5, w=[0.5, 0.5] gives compound = 0.4375 (not 0.50)
2. Two observations at c=0.5, w=[1.0, 1.0] gives compound = 0.75 (unchanged, backward compat)
3. Single observation at c=0.8, w=0.5 gives compound = 0.4 (weighted down)
4. Observation with w*c > 1.0 is clamped (no negative probabilities)
5. Correlated case (correlation=1.0) unchanged: max(c*w)
6. Interpolated case (0 < correlation < 1) uses corrected independent formula
7. Test: test_compound_confidence_weighted() with known inputs/outputs
8. Test: test_compound_confidence_unweighted() unchanged (backward compat)
close_reason: Fixed weighted compound_confidence formula from (1-c)^w to linear scaling 1-Π(1-w*c). The old formula had no probabilistic interpretation. New formula correctly combines weighted probabilities: P=1-Π(1-w_i*c_i). Tests: 12 compound_confidence tests pass including bug fix verification and weight clamping.

title: Markov Chains: evaluate for state-transition modeling and absorbing-state risk analysis
description: OHM currently has Bayesian networks (conditional inference), Monte Carlo (stochastic propagation), and temporal decay (time-based degradation). Markov Chains would add a complementary capability: state-transition modeling where nodes have discrete states that evolve probabilistically over time. Specific use cases: (1) Absorbing Markov Chains for risk analysis — what's the probability a system eventually reaches a failure state? (2) Steady-state analysis for long-run behavior of recurring processes. (3) Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) for optimal action selection under uncertainty — given current state and available actions, what's the optimal policy? This is distinct from Bayesian inference (which conditions on evidence) and Monte Carlo (which does one-shot propagation). The evaluation should determine: does DuckDB support the linear algebra needed (matrix multiplication for transition matrices), or should this use numpy/scipy? Should it be a substrate method or an agent capability? Reference: Grinstead & Snell's 'Introduction to Probability' Ch. 11 on Markov Chains.
design:
Research spike — do NOT implement yet. Evaluate:

1. Use case fit: Which OHM scenarios benefit from Markov Chains?
   - Supply chain: state transitions (operational → disrupted → recovered)
   - Medical: disease progression (healthy → symptomatic → critical)
   - Cybersecurity: attack chain progression (recon → exploit → exfil)
   - Cattle: herd health state transitions

2. Technical feasibility:
   - DuckDB can multiply matrices via Python UDFs or list operations
   - Better approach: numpy/scipy for transition matrix math, DuckDB for data storage
   - Absorbing Markov Chain fundamental matrix: N = (I - Q)^(-1)
   - Expected steps to absorption: t = N * 1
   - Absorption probabilities: B = N * R

3. API design sketch:
   def markov_absorbing_risk(conn, start_node, *, edge_types=['CAUSES', 'TRANSITIONS_TO']):
       # Build transition matrix from edges
       # Identify absorbing states (nodes with no outgoing edges)
       # Compute absorption probabilities
       return {target: prob for target in absorbing_states}

4. Comparison with existing methods:
   - Bayesian: P(effect|cause) — conditional, static
   - Monte Carlo: P(reach|source) — stochastic, one-shot
   - Markov: P(reach_absorbing|start) — sequential, multi-step
   - Markov fills the gap: 'what happens over time with repeated transitions?'

5. Recommendation: P2 research spike. If feasible, implement as substrate method in methods.py. If DuckDB linear algebra is insufficient, use numpy with a DuckDB→numpy→DuckDB bridge.

Deliverable: markov-feasibility.md in docs/ with findings and recommendation.
acceptance_criteria:
1. markov-feasibility.md written with use case analysis
2. Technical feasibility assessed (DuckDB vs numpy)
3. API design sketch with example usage
4. Comparison table: Markov vs Bayesian vs Monte Carlo
5. Go/no-go recommendation with rationale
6. If go: implementation plan with file locations and dependencies
7. If no-go: alternative approaches to fill the sequential-state gap
close_reason: Research spike complete. Created docs/markov-feasibility.md with: (1) Use case analysis showing high fit for supply chain, medical, cybersecurity, cattle health, (2) Technical feasibility: DuckDB insufficient for matrix ops, recommend NumPy bridge architecture, (3) API design sketch with markov_absorbing_risk(), markov_expected_steps(), (4) Comparison table vs Bayesian vs Monte Carlo, (5) GO recommendation with 4-phase implementation plan. No implementation yet — this is a research spike deliverable.

title: /voi should include DEPENDS_ON by default — add DEPENDS_ON to default edge_types in compute_voi
SDK connect_http() unicode bug — non-ASCII characters in responses cause decode errors
description:
The SDK's connect_http() function makes HTTP requests to the ohmd daemon and parses JSON responses. When responses contain non-ASCII characters (e.g., node labels with Unicode like 'café', 'naïve', or emoji), the response decoding fails with a UnicodeDecodeError.

This happens because the HTTP response's Content-Type header may not specify charset=utf-8, or the response body contains Latin-1 characters that Python's default JSON decoder can't handle.

The fix should:
1. Force UTF-8 decoding of response bodies in connect_http()
2. Add a fallback to Latin-1 if UTF-8 fails (with a warning log)
3. Ensure all server endpoints set Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
4. Add a test with Unicode node labels to verify round-trip encoding

This is a P2 because most OHM data is ASCII, but it blocks internationalization and any domain with non-English terminology.
design:
Fix in sdk.py connect_http():
1. After response = requests.get/post(...), use:
   response.encoding = 'utf-8'  # Force UTF-8
2. If json() still fails with UnicodeDecodeError:
   try:
       response.encoding = 'latin-1'
       data = response.json()
       logger.warning(f'Latin-1 fallback used for response from {url}')
   except:
       raise

Server fix in server.py:
1. All _json_response() calls should include:
   self.send_header('Content-Type', 'application/json; charset=utf-8')

Test: test_sdk_unicode.py or add to test_sdk.py:
- Create node with label 'café'
- Read node back via SDK
- Verify label is 'café' not 'cafÃ©'
acceptance_criteria:
1. Node with Unicode label 'café' round-trips correctly through SDK
2. Node with emoji label '⚠️ Risk' round-trips correctly
3. Latin-1 fallback works when server sends wrong encoding
4. Warning is logged when fallback is used
5. All server JSON responses include charset=utf-8
6. Test: test_unicode_roundtrip() in test_sdk.py
close_reason: Fixed: (1) SDK connect_http() now forces UTF-8 decoding with Latin-1 fallback and warning log, (2) server.py _json_response() now sends charset=utf-8, (3) Added 3 Unicode round-trip tests (café, emoji, mixed). All 140 SDK tests pass.

title: DuckLake auto-restore on startup — if ohm_nodes empty, pull from DuckLake
description:
When the local DuckDB database is empty (0 rows in ohm_nodes), the daemon should automatically attempt to restore from DuckLake. This handles the case where the local database was lost due to disk failure, corruption beyond WAL recovery, or fresh deployment.

DuckLake is OHM's lakehouse storage layer (see docs/ducklake.md). It stores periodic snapshots of the knowledge graph in object storage (S3/MinIO). The restore process should:
1. On startup, check if ohm_nodes is empty
2. If empty, query DuckLake for the most recent snapshot
3. Download and attach the snapshot as a DuckDB database
4. Copy all tables from snapshot to local database
5. Log the restore with snapshot timestamp and row counts
6. If no snapshot exists, proceed with empty database (fresh start)

This should be automatic — no manual intervention required. The daemon should log prominently that a restore occurred so operators are aware.
design:
Restore flow in db.py initialize_schema() or server startup:
1. After schema init, run: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ohm_nodes
2. If count == 0:
   a. Log 'Empty database detected, attempting DuckLake restore'
   b. Try: ATTACH 's3://ohm-ducklake/snapshots/latest.duckdb' AS ducklake (READ_ONLY)
   c. If attach succeeds:
      - Copy ohm_nodes, ohm_edges, ohm_observations, ohm_agent_state, ohm_agent_config
      - Copy ohm_meta (preserve schema version)
      - Log row counts for each table
      - DETACH ducklake
   d. If attach fails (no snapshots, network error):
      - Log 'No DuckLake snapshot available — starting fresh'
      - Proceed with empty database
3. Return connection

Configuration: DuckLake S3 credentials from /etc/ohm/ohmd.json or environment variables.
Test: test_ducklake.py already exists — add test_auto_restore_on_empty().
acceptance_criteria:
1. Empty database triggers automatic DuckLake restore on startup
2. All tables are restored with correct row counts
3. Schema version in ohm_meta is preserved
4. If no snapshot exists, daemon starts with empty database (no crash)
5. Restore is logged with snapshot timestamp and row counts
6. If S3 is unreachable, daemon starts with empty database (no crash)
7. Test: create empty DB, mock DuckLake snapshot, verify restore
8. Test: create empty DB, no snapshot, verify clean start
close_reason: DUPLICATE of OHM-6cz. DuckLake auto-restore on startup is tracked under OHM-6cz.

title: WAL corruption recovery — detect and recover from DuckDB WAL corruption on startup
description:
DuckDB's Write-Ahead Log (WAL) can become corrupted after an unclean shutdown (power loss, OOM kill, SIGKILL). When this happens, DuckDB refuses to open the database with a 'WAL corruption' or 'database is invalid' error. The ohmd daemon fails to start, and all agents lose access to the knowledge graph.

This issue covers detection and recovery:
1. On startup, catch DuckDB connection errors and check for WAL corruption patterns
2. If WAL is corrupted, attempt recovery by:
   a. Backing up the corrupted database file
   b. Deleting the WAL file (DuckDB can recover committed data without it)
   c. If that fails, restoring from the most recent DuckLake snapshot
3. Log the recovery action with correlation_id for audit trail
4. Alert agents that data may have been lost (last N seconds of writes)

Reference: DuckDB's WAL is at <db_path>.wal. Deleting it is safe for committed transactions but loses uncommitted writes. See docs/crash-recovery-notes.md.
design:
Recovery flow in db.py get_connection():
1. Try duckdb.connect(db_path) normally
2. Catch duckdb.IOException / duckdb.Error
3. Check error message for 'WAL', 'corrupt', 'invalid', 'recovery'
4. If WAL-related:
   a. Log warning with correlation_id
   b. Copy db_path to db_path.corrupted.{timestamp}
   c. Delete db_path.wal
   d. Retry connection
   e. If still failing, attempt DuckLake restore (OHM-6mv.14)
5. Return connection or raise DaemonNotRunningError with recovery details

Test: test_wal_recovery.py already exists — add test for startup recovery path.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Corrupted WAL file is detected on startup and automatically recovered
2. Original database is backed up before recovery attempt
3. Recovery is logged with correlation_id
4. If WAL deletion succeeds, daemon starts normally
5. If WAL deletion fails, falls through to DuckLake restore
6. Test: create corrupted WAL, verify recovery deletes it and daemon starts
7. Test: verify backup file exists after recovery
close_reason: DUPLICATE of OHM-8n9. WAL corruption recovery is tracked under OHM-8n9 which also covers the empty-table auto-restore gap.

title: ohm voi CLI command — expose VoI analysis to agents and humans
description: Add 'ohm voi' CLI command that wraps the /voi endpoint. Usage: 'ohm voi —top 10 —agent metis' returns ranked research priorities for Métis. 'ohm voi —decision and_or_conversion' returns VoI for specific decision node. 'ohm voi —format json|markdown' for output format. Integrates with ohm graph CLI.

title: Source reliability weighting for observations — agents with higher accuracy get more weight
description: When computing compound confidence, weight observations by source reliability. Agents with higher accuracy (from source_reliability tracking) contribute more to confidence. An observation from a reliable source (p_accurate=0.9) should count more than one from an unreliable source (p_accurate=0.5). Already have /source_reliability and record_outcome endpoints — wire them into confidence computation.
design: compound_confidence now: weighted_geometric_mean(observation_values, weights=source_reliabilities). If source_reliability not available, default weight=0.5. This means: observations from reliable agents (0.9) count 1.8× more than unknown agents (0.5). VoI uses source-weighted confidence, so research from reliable agents reduces uncertainty more per observation.

title: Agent task assignment — auto-generate research tasks from VoI rankings
description: Wire /voi output into OHM task system. High-VoI nodes auto-generate research tasks assigned to agents by expertise match. For each under-observed node: compute gap_score=(1-mean_confidence)×downstream_impact. Match to agents via tag overlap. Create task with: node_id, gap description, suggested agent, VoI score, priority. Agents pick up tasks via list_tasks endpoint.
design: New endpoint: POST /voi/tasks?agent=metis&top=5. Returns ranked research targets. Each target: node_id, label, current_observations, gap_score, downstream_decisions_affected, suggested_research, VoI_score, matched_tags. Agent expertise map from registered agent tags. Clio → research tags, Hephaestus → security/code tags, Socrates → philosophy/critical-thinking tags.
close_reason: generate_voi_tasks with gap scoring, capability matching, and workload awareness

title: Temporal confidence decay — observations age, confidence decays over time
description: Model observation aging: confidence in a claim decays over time. When an observation is 30 days old, it should carry less weight than one from 30 minutes ago. Uses exponential decay: confidence(t) = initial_confidence × e^(-λ × days_since). Lambda configurable per domain (Hormuz: fast decay, trap research: slow decay). Already designed in TOPO L4 (Bayesian updating) — port the math.
design: Add decay_lambda field to nodes (default 0.01/day = 30-day half-life). Compute effective_confidence = confidence × e^(-lambda × days_since_last_observation). VoI uses effective_confidence instead of raw confidence, so stale observations increase uncertainty and increase VoI of fresh research. Reference: 202605031400_Bayesian_L4_Updating_for_TOPO.md in Métis zettelkasten.

title: Probability distributions on edges — replace confidence point estimates with P05/P50/P95
description: Replace edge confidence (0-1 point estimate) with probability distributions. Hubberd's key insight: EVI requires preserving full distributions, not expected values. Add: confidence_p05, confidence_p50, confidence_p95 fields on edges. Compound confidence computes geometric mean of P50 values. Sensitivity uses full distribution for Monte Carlo propagation. Backward compatible: existing edges default P50=current confidence, P05=P50*0.8, P95=min(1,P50*1.2).
design: Schema migration adds confidence_p05, confidence_p50, confidence_p95 to edges. Existing edges: confidence_p50 = confidence, P05/P95 derived from sigma if available, else 0.8×/1.2× heuristic. API: /edge confidence parameter now accepts either float (backward compat) or {p05, p50, p95} object. /sensitivity and /inference use P50 for point estimates, full distribution for VoI calculations.
notes: Per ADR-013: PERT three-point estimation protocol. Add probability_p05, probability_p50, probability_p95 columns to ohm_edges. Derive probability = (O + 4M + P) / 6. Store variance = ((P - O) / 6)² for VoI. Backwards compatible: existing probability values remain valid as P50 defaults.

title: Bayesian: truncation at max_nodes silently drops nodes arbitrarily
description: When the network exceeds max_nodes (default 50), build_bayesian_network() truncates with node_ids = set(list(node_ids)[:max_nodes]). This takes the first 50 nodes from a set, which has arbitrary iteration order in Python. The truncation should be deterministic and meaningful — e.g., keep nodes with the most edges, highest centrality, or closest to the root_nodes scope. Currently it silently drops random nodes, which can produce different Bayesian networks on different runs with the same data.

title: Bayesian: confounder perturbation formula is heuristic, not derived
description: In compute_sensitivity(), the confounder perturbation analysis uses a heuristic formula: bias = conf_strength^2 * min(RR-1, ATE) * 0.5. This is not derived from any standard sensitivity analysis method (e.g., VanderWeele & Ding, Cinelli & Hazlett). The quadratic scaling and 0.5 factor are arbitrary. The perturbation should use the standard E-value bounding approach: for a given confounder strength s, the bias-adjusted RR is bounded by RR / s and RR * s, giving a proper sensitivity interval rather than an ad-hoc quadratic decay.

title: Bayesian: root node prior defaults to 0.3 with no justification
description: In build_bayesian_network(), root nodes get a default prior of 0.3 (30% probability of 'bad' state) when no observations exist. This is an arbitrary constant with no theoretical justification. The prior should be configurable (like leak_probability and default_probability already are) and ideally derived from domain knowledge or node metadata. A hardcoded 0.3 prior biases all root nodes toward 'good' regardless of context.

title: Add edge deduplication to write_edge and neighborhood queries
BUG: DuckLake snapshots() function name may be incorrect
description:
store.py list_snapshots() queries {alias}.snapshots() but DuckDB's DuckLake extension uses ducklake_snapshots('{alias}') as the function name. Currently returning 5 snapshots which seems to work, but this may be DuckDB resolving a different function than intended.

If snapshots are actually being read from a different source, time-travel queries (graph/at) will query the wrong data.

Need to verify: are the 5 snapshots real DuckLake snapshots or artifacts from a different query?

Correct syntax per DuckDB docs: SELECT * FROM ducklake_snapshots('ohm_lake')
acceptance_criteria:
1. list_snapshots uses ducklake_snapshots('{alias}') instead of {alias}.snapshots()
2. Returned snapshots are verifiable DuckLake snapshots with real data
notes: Updated list_snapshots() to use ducklake_snapshots(alias) as primary query with fallback to alias.snapshots() for older DuckLake builds; validated with tests/test_ducklake.py (23 passed).
close_reason: Fixed: list_snapshots() now uses ducklake_snapshots() syntax. Verified: returns 19+ snapshots.

title: CLEANUP: Fix 46 ruff lint errors across src/ohm
notes: Ruff clean on current main baseline: python -m ruff check src/ohm passes with no findings.

title: SIGPIPE handling in daemon
description: The ohmd daemon doesn't handle SIGPIPE signals. When a client disconnects mid-write, the daemon may crash with a broken pipe error. Need to: 1) Install SIGPIPE handler (ignore on Unix), 2) Catch BrokenPipeError in write paths, 3) Log and continue instead of crashing. Note: SIGPIPE is Unix-only; Windows uses different error handling.

title: ADR-011: Observation type extensibility
description: Write ADR documenting the design decision for observation type extensibility. Currently VALID_OBSERVATION_TYPES is a frozenset in schema.py. The ADR should document: why observation types are validated, how domains extend them (SchemaConfig), the lifecycle (experimental → registered → canonical per ADR-007), and how custom observation types interact with the SDK observe() method and CLI. This completes the ADR coverage for Phase 2 features.

title: FEATURE: OHM-kdk.4 DuckLake WAL corruption recovery — auto-restore from snapshot
description:
When DuckDB WAL corruption is detected, automatically restore from the most recent DuckLake snapshot instead of losing data.

Implementation steps:
1. Detect corruption on startup or during writes:
   - DuckDB raises fatal error on WAL corruption
   - Catch exception, check if DuckLake snapshot exists
   - If yes, trigger recovery flow

2. Recovery flow:
   a. Read latest DuckLake snapshot_id
   b. Export graph state from DuckLake at that snapshot:
      SELECT * FROM ohm_lake.ohm_nodes AT (VERSION => snapshot_id)
      SELECT * FROM ohm_lake.ohm_edges AT (VERSION => snapshot_id)
   c. Create new DuckDB database with OHM schema
   d. Import all nodes and edges from snapshot
   e. Start ohmd with new DB
   f. Re-sync from DuckLake (to pick up any changes after snapshot)

3. Optional: Add /admin/restore endpoint to trigger manual restore:
   POST /admin/restore?snapshot=N
   Also: GET /admin/recovery-status (shows: last snapshot, last sync, corruption count)

4. Add corruption counter to ohmd stats:
   ohm_change_feed records recoveries (count, timestamp, snapshot_id, rows_restored)

5. Periodic compaction to reduce Parquet fragmentation:
   CALL ducklake_merge_adjacent_files('ohm_lake')

Requires: OHM-kdk.1, OHM-kdk.2, OHM-kdk.3

Estimated: ~150 lines
acceptance_criteria:
1. WAL corruption on startup triggers automatic recovery (no manual rm -f)
2. Recovery completes in <30 seconds for current dataset size
3. All nodes and edges from snapshot are restored
4. Post-recovery sync picks up any changes after snapshot
5. Recovery statistics logged (when, which snapshot, rows_restored)
6. Compaction callable without downtime

title: FEATURE: OHM-kdk.3 DuckLake time-travel queries — /admin/snapshots, /graph/at?version=N
description:
Add API endpoints to query historical graph state using DuckLake snapshots.

Implementation steps:
1. Add GET /admin/snapshots — list available DuckLake snapshots:
   SELECT snapshot_id, snapshot_time, commit_message 
   FROM ducklake_snapshots('ohm_lake')
   ORDER BY snapshot_time DESC
   Returns: [{id, timestamp, message}]

2. Add GET /graph/at?version=N — query graph at specific snapshot:
   SELECT * FROM ohm_lake.ohm_nodes AT (VERSION => N)
   SELECT * FROM ohm_lake.ohm_edges AT (VERSION => N)
   Returns full graph state (nodes + edges) at snapshot N

3. Optionally: GET /graph/changes?from_version=M&to_version=N
   Returns insertions/deletions between two snapshots:
   SELECT * FROM ducklake_table_insertions('ohm_lake', 'ohm_nodes', M, N)
   SELECT * FROM ducklake_table_deletions('ohm_lake', 'ohm_nodes', M, N)

Requires: OHM-kdk.1 (DuckLake attached) and OHM-kdk.2 (sync running).

Estimated: ~80 lines
acceptance_criteria:
1. /admin/snapshots returns list of snapshots with id and timestamp
2. /graph/at?version=N returns full graph state as of snapshot N
3. Snapshot list shows all historical snapshots (at least 1 per write)
4. Graph at v1 returns state before any changes (empty or near-empty)
5. Graph at latest snapshot returns current state (matches /stats)

title: FEATURE: OHM-kdk.2 DuckLake sync interval — periodic dual-write
description:
Implement periodic sync from DuckDB to DuckLake on configurable interval. Writes go to DuckDB immediately (hot path), then sync to DuckLake on interval.

Implementation steps:
1. Add sync_interval to DuckLake config (default: 60 seconds)
2. Add last_push/last_pull timestamps to ohm_agent_state table (if not already there)
3. Modify store.py sync_heartbeat() to:
   - Read last_push timestamp for this agent
   - Query ohm_change_feed for changes since last_push
   - Write each change to DuckLake ohm_change_feed table
   - Record new last_push timestamp
4. Add a background sync thread or integrate with existing heartbeat loop:
   - Ohmd daemon calls sync_heartbeat() every sync_interval seconds
   - If no changes since last_push, no-op (fast)
5. Handle first-time setup: if no last_push exists, push full change feed

Existing code: push_to_ducklake() and pull_from_ducklake() exist in store.py but need:
- Schema alignment (DuckLake uses different column names)
- Timestamp tracking (last_push/last_pull)
- Error handling for DuckLake-specific failures

Estimated: ~150 lines
acceptance_criteria:
1. Changes written to DuckDB appear in DuckLake within sync_interval seconds
2. Sync is idempotent (running twice produces same result)
3. Changes from multiple agents all sync correctly
4. First-time sync pushes full change feed history
5. No data loss: if DuckDB corrupts, DuckLake has all changes since last_push

title: FEATURE: OHM-kdk.1 DuckLake extension + catalog setup
description:
Load DuckLake extension in db.py, attach DuckLake catalog on daemon start, create OHM mirror tables without PKs.

Implementation steps:
1. Add DuckLake to _load_extensions() in db.py: INSTALL ducklake FROM core; LOAD ducklake
2. Add OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH and OHM_DUCKLAKE_DATA config keys to ohmd.json (with defaults)
3. In store.py __init__ or connect(), ATTACH ducklake catalog if configured:
   ATTACH 'ducklake:{OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH}' AS ohm_lake (DATA_PATH '{OHM_DUCKLAKE_DATA}')
4. Create mirror tables in ohm_lake schema: ohm_nodes, ohm_edges, ohm_observations
   - Same columns as DuckDB tables but NO PRIMARY KEY (DuckLake constraint)
   - Use VARCHAR for all columns (no type constraints)
5. Validate that ohmd starts cleanly with DuckLake attached

Config additions to ohmd.json:
{
  "ducklake": {
    "path": "/var/lib/ohm/ohm_lake.ducklake",
    "data_path": "/var/lib/ohm/ohm_lake_data",
    "sync_interval_seconds": 60
  }
}

Key constraint: DuckLake does NOT support PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE.
Node/edge uniqueness must be enforced in application code (ohmd already does via upsert logic).

Estimated: ~100 lines
acceptance_criteria:
1. DuckLake extension loads without error on ohmd start
2. DuckLake catalog attaches and DATA_PATH creates Parquet files on write
3. Mirror tables created without PKs but with all OHM columns
4. ohmd starts cleanly with ducklake config present
5. Manual write to DuckLake works (verified via ducklake_list_files)

title: FEATURE: DuckLake as persistent store — durability, snapshots, and recovery
description:
Use DuckLake (DuckDB's lakehouse format) as OHM's persistent store, with DuckDB as the hot cache. This solves the WAL corruption problem permanently and adds snapshot/time-travel capabilities.

Architecture:
  Write: ohmd → DuckDB (immediate) → DuckLake (periodic sync)
  Read: ohmd → DuckDB (cached, same as now)
  Recovery: DuckLake snapshot → DuckDB (on WAL corruption)

What DuckLake gives OHM:
1. Snapshots: Every write auto-creates a snapshot → time-travel queries, recovery from corruption, audit trail
2. Durability: Data in Parquet files, not just WAL → no data loss on schema migration
3. Change tracking: Built-in insertions/deletions → replaces ad-hoc ohm_change_feed
4. Future path: Multi-writer via DuckLake catalog (agents write directly)

Constraints:
- DuckLake doesn't support PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE → enforce in app code (ohmd already does)
- DuckLake is batch-oriented → not a replacement for hot cache
- Parquet file fragmentation on small writes → periodic compaction needed
- DuckLake extension is v1.0 → may have edge cases

Configuration:
- OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH=/var/lib/ohm/ohm_lake.ducklake (catalog)
- OHM_DUCKLAKE_DATA=/var/lib/ohm/ohm_lake_data (Parquet files)
- OHM_DUCKLAKE_SYNC_INTERVAL=60 (seconds)

Existing code: push_to_ducklake() and pull_from_ducklake() already exist in store.py but need schema alignment and activation. The sync_heartbeat() method already calls them when OHM_DUCKLAKE_PATH is set.

Estimated effort: ~200-300 lines of changes to db.py, schema.py, store.py, server.py, and ohmd.json.
acceptance_criteria:
1. DuckLake extension loaded and catalog attached on daemon start
2. All writes dual-written to DuckDB (immediate) and DuckLake (sync interval)
3. Recovery from DuckLake snapshot after WAL corruption (no data loss)
4. Time-travel queries via /admin/snapshots and /graph/at?version=N
5. No PRIMARY KEY constraints in DuckLake tables (enforced in app)
6. Periodic sync (configurable interval, default 60s)
7. WAL corruption → automatic recovery from last DuckLake snapshot
close_reason: Replaced with scoped sub-tasks: OHM-kdk.1 through OHM-kdk.4

title: BUG: /listen without 'since' returns 500 — agent_state.last_sync is datetime not string
description:
OHM-9ud attempted to fix /listen without 'since' parameter by adding a fallback to agent_state.last_sync. However, when agent_state.last_sync exists, it's a datetime object, not a string. The code does:

  since = state['last_sync']

This passes a datetime object directly to query_change_feed, which expects a string and does regex matching on it, causing:

  TypeError: expected string or bytes-like object, got 'datetime.datetime'

This is a regression from OHM-9ud — before the fix, /listen without since returned 400 (validation error). After the fix, it returns 500 (internal error).

Fix: convert datetime to string before passing to query_change_feed:
  since = state['last_sync'].isoformat() if isinstance(state['last_sync'], datetime) else state['last_sync']

OR: if no 'since' provided and no last_sync, default to recent changes (last hour or last 24 hours) instead of raising an error.
acceptance_criteria:
1. /listen without 'since' returns 200 with recent changes (last 24h default)
2. If agent_state.last_sync is datetime, convert to .isoformat() string
3. No 500 errors on any /listen call

title: BUG: /listen without 'since' parameter returns 500 instead of 400 validation error
description:
GET /listen without the required 'since' parameter returns 500 Internal Server Error instead of 400 Bad Request with a clear validation message.

Error: 'expected string or bytes-like object, got datetime.datetime'

The server is trying to parse the missing 'since' value and hitting a type error. It should validate the parameter before processing.

Note: The SDK OHMClient.listen() has 'since' as optional (since: str | None = None), which suggests the API might intend to support listening without a timestamp (e.g., get the most recent N changes). If so, this is a missing feature rather than a validation bug.

Impact: Calling /listen without 'since' crashes the server with an unhandled exception.
acceptance_criteria:
1. /listen without 'since' returns 400 with clear validation message, OR
2. /listen without 'since' returns the most recent N changes (default limit)
3. No 500 errors on missing parameters

title: BUG: SDK OHMClient create_edge() uses from_node/to_node but API expects from/to
description:
The SDK OHMClient create_edge() method sends field names from_node and to_node in the JSON body, but the /edge API endpoint expects from and to.

Testing:
- POST /edge {from_node: ..., to_node: ..., type: ..., ...} → 400 Bad Request
- POST /edge {from: ..., to: ..., type: ..., ...} → 201 Created

The shared ohm_client.py correctly uses 'from' and 'to' (matching the API). The SDK client uses 'from_node' and 'to_node' which the server rejects.

Impact: The SDK client cannot create edges. Agents using the SDK get 400 errors on every edge creation attempt.

Additionally, the SDK OHMClient is missing many methods that the shared client and HttpGraph provide: search, neighborhood, observe, challenge, support, confidence, impact, path, delete_node, delete_edge, get_node, get_edge, list_nodes, create_batch, health, schema, update_state.
acceptance_criteria:
1. SDK create_edge() sends 'from' and 'to' (matching the API)
2. All SDK method parameter names match the API field names
3. SDK client parity with shared ohm_client.py for commonly used methods
close_reason: Fixed: SDK create_edge() sends 'from'/'to' matching the API.

title: BUG: SDK OHMClient.register() parameters don't match /register API
description:
The SDK OHMClient (ohm.client.OHMClient) register() method has completely different parameter names than the /register API endpoint. Neither can successfully register an agent.

SDK register() signature:
  register(self, *, description, values, goals, capabilities, interests, listens_to)

API /register expects:
  {name, values, goals, skills, services}

Problems:
1. SDK has no 'name' parameter — the only required field for registration
2. SDK 'capabilities' doesn't map to API 'skills'
3. SDK 'interests' and 'listens_to' have no API equivalent
4. SDK 'description' doesn't map to any API field (same as OHM-9i0)
5. The shared client (ohm_client.py) has the correct signature: register(name, values, goals, skills, services)

Calling SDK register() with 'name' raises: TypeError: got an unexpected keyword argument 'name'

Impact: The SDK client cannot register agents. Agents must use the shared ohm_client.py or call the HTTP API directly.
acceptance_criteria:
1. SDK register() accepts: name (required), values, goals, skills, services
2. All parameters match the /register API
3. Register test passes with both SDK and shared client using same call signature
close_reason: Fixed: SDK register() now accepts name, values, goals, skills matching the API.

title: FEATURE: Add create-only and find-or-create semantics for POST /node
description:
POST /node with an existing ID does upsert (returns 200, updates the node). This is reasonable but creates problems:

1. No way to do 'create only if not exists' — two agents creating the same concept silently overwrite each other
2. No /node/find_or_create endpoint — the URL returns 404
3. No atomic 'create or get' operation — agents must check-then-create, which is a race condition

Current behavior:
- POST /node {id: 'x', ...} → 201 (first creation)
- POST /node {id: 'x', ...} → 200 (updates existing node)
- POST /node/find_or_create → 404

Desired behavior:
- POST /node → upsert (current, documented)
- POST /node?create_only=true → 201 or 409 Conflict
- POST /node/find_or_create → return existing node by label+type match, or create new

This is important for multi-agent idempotency — agents need to safely create concepts without overwriting each other's data.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Document POST /node upsert behavior
2. Add create_only query parameter (returns 409 if ID exists)
3. Add /node/find_or_create endpoint (match by label+type, return existing or create)
4. All three behaviors tested in test suite

title: BUG: Duplicate node ID creates second node instead of rejecting or updating
description:
Creating a node with an ID that already exists succeeds (201) instead of returning 409 Conflict or updating the existing node.

Steps to reproduce:
1. POST /node {id: 'test_node', label: 'test', type: 'concept'}
2. POST /node {id: 'test_node', label: 'duplicate', type: 'concept'}
3. Second request succeeds (201) — creates a second node with the same ID

Wait, on re-check: the second node gets a different ID (auto-generated from the label). So the issue is that the id field is being ignored or overridden when it conflicts. Let me verify... Actually, the server generates a deterministic ID from the label when no id is provided, and the id field in the request body may be silently dropped if it conflicts.

The real issue: if two agents create nodes with the same label, they get different auto-generated IDs. There's no idempotency guarantee for node creation. The register endpoint has idempotent registration (OHM-5n7), but regular node creation doesn't.

Impact: Agents creating the same concept independently get duplicate nodes. No way to do 'create if not exists' atomically.
acceptance_criteria:
1. If a node with the same ID already exists, return 409 Conflict
2. If a node with the same label and type already exists, optionally return the existing node (find_or_create semantics)
3. Document the idempotency behavior of POST /node
close_reason: Updated: POST /node with existing ID does upsert (200), not duplicate creation. Real issue is no create-only option and no find_or_create endpoint.

title: BUG: /confidence endpoint returns empty challenges/supports for nodes with CHALLENGED_BY edges
description:
The GET /confidence/{node_id} endpoint returns empty arrays for challenges, supports, and refinements even when the node has edges of those types.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Create node A with a CHALLENGED_BY edge pointing to it
2. GET /confidence/A
3. Returns: {original: None, challenges: [], supports: [], refinements: []}

The and-or-conversion node has CHALLENGED_BY and REFINES edges, but /confidence returns all empty. This may be a query direction issue (the edges point FROM other nodes TO the target, but the confidence endpoint may be looking at outgoing edges only).

Impact: Agents can't use /confidence to audit the evidence chain for a node's claims. The challenge/support/refine workflow is broken for downstream consumers.
acceptance_criteria:
1. /confidence/{node_id} returns all CHALLENGED_BY edges pointing TO the node
2. /confidence/{node_id} returns all SUPPORTS edges pointing TO the node
3. /confidence/{node_id} returns all REFINES edges pointing TO or FROM the node
4. Each entry includes the source node, edge confidence, and provenance
close_reason: Fixed: /confidence returns challenges and refinements. Verified: 1 challenge, 5 refinements on AND→OR.

title: BUG: /schema edge_types returns layer names instead of edge type names
description:
The GET /schema endpoint returns layer names (L1, L2, L3, L4) under the 'edge_types' key instead of actual edge type names (APPLIES_TO, VALUES, GOALS, CHALLENGED_BY, etc.).

Current response:
  edge_types: {L1: {...}, L2: {...}, L3: {...}, L4: {...}}
  
Expected response:
  edge_types: {APPLIES_TO: {...}, VALUES: {...}, GOALS: {...}, CHALLENGED_BY: {...}, ...}

The actual edge type names are in the 'layers' key (which also returns L1-L4). This is confusing — two keys returning the same data, and neither returning what the key name promises.

Impact: Agents using /schema to discover valid edge types get layer names instead. They'd need to query the graph or read documentation separately to find valid edge types like APPLIES_TO, VALUES, REFINES, CHALLENGED_BY, etc.
acceptance_criteria:
1. /schema edge_types returns actual edge type names (APPLIES_TO, VALUES, GOALS, CAPABLE_OF, CHALLENGED_BY, SUPPORTS, REFINES, etc.)
2. /schema layers returns layer definitions (L1, L2, L3, L4) with their descriptions
3. Both are distinct and correctly labeled
close_reason: Fixed: /schema edge_types returns flat list of actual edge types (APPLIES_TO, AWAITING, etc.).

title: BUG: Shared ohm_client.py register() has 'description' parameter that doesn't exist in API
description:
The shared ohm_client.py at /root/olympus/shared/ohm_client.py has a register() method with signature:
  register(name, description=None, values=None, goals=None, skills=None)

But the /register API endpoint expects:
  {name, values, goals, skills, services}

The 'description' parameter doesn't map to any API field, and calling register() with a description kwarg raises TypeError. The API puts the description in the 'content' field of the agent node, not a separate parameter.

Additionally, the SDK's OHMClient.register() uses keyword-only args and maps correctly to the API. The shared client should match.

Impact: Agents using the shared client get confusing errors when trying to register with a description.
acceptance_criteria:
1. ohm_client.py register() matches the API: name, values, goals, skills, services (all keyword)
2. Description maps to the node 'content' field (or a 'content' parameter is added)
3. No TypeError when calling with documented parameters
close_reason: Fixed: shared ohm_client.py register() uses keyword-only args matching the API. No 'description' parameter.

title: BUG: SDK HttpGraph.search() method missing — should be search() not search_nodes()
description:
The SDK's HttpGraph class exposes search_nodes() but not search(). This is inconsistent with:
1. The shared ohm_client.py which uses g.search()
2. The REST API which uses GET /search
3. User expectations — 'search' is the natural verb

Additionally, the shared ohm_client.py (at /root/olympus/shared/ohm_client.py) is the client that agents actually use, and it has search(). The SDK client (ohm.client.OHMClient) has search_nodes(). This inconsistency means code written for one client doesn't work with the other.

Impact: Agents using the SDK client need to remember to call search_nodes() instead of search(). This is a minor friction but causes confusion.
acceptance_criteria:
1. HttpGraph has both search() and search_nodes() as aliases (or search_nodes() is an alias for search())
2. Both methods have identical behavior and return type
3. Shared ohm_client.py and SDK client use the same method name
close_reason: Fixed: SDK HttpGraph has search() method. Shared client has search_nodes() alias.

title: BUG: Enriched listen doesn't include data for node INSERT operations
description:
