When using /listen?enrich=true, edge INSERT operations include a 'data' field with from_node, to_node, edge_type, and layer. However, node INSERT operations do NOT include the 'data' field — the enriched payload is null/missing for node creates.

This means agents listening for changes cannot see what node was created without making a separate GET /node/{id} call. This partially defeats the purpose of the enriched change feed.

Expected: node INSERTs should include data with label, type, content, created_by (same fields returned by GET /node/{id}).
Actual: node INSERTs have data=null or omit the data field entirely.

Impact: Agents cannot efficiently react to new nodes in the change feed. They must make a follow-up call to get node details, doubling API calls for every node creation event.

title: BUG: active_agents resets to 0 after daemon restart — change feed is ephemeral
description:
The active_agents count in /stats is derived from the change feed — it counts distinct agent_names from recent writes. But the change feed is stored in DuckDB (not persisted separately), and when the daemon restarts (especially after WAL corruption), the change feed is empty.

This means:
- After restart: active_agents = 0
- After one write: active_agents = 1 (only the most recent writer)
- Historical activity is lost

The fix in OHM-8gu changed active_agents from counting synced agents to counting change feed writers. But the change feed is ephemeral — it's a WAL-backed table that gets truncated on restart.

Impact: /stats active_agents is unreliable as a metric. It undercounts after restarts and doesn't reflect historical activity.
acceptance_criteria:
1. active_agents persists across daemon restarts
2. Consider using a separate table (agent_activity) that tracks last-seen timestamps per agent
3. OR use the ohm_agents table with a last_active_at timestamp that updates on each write
4. /stats active_agents reflects all agents that have been active in the last 24 hours (configurable)

title: FEATURE: OHMClient Python package — pip-installable with config from ohm-config.json
description:
The shared OHMClient at /root/olympus/shared/ohm_client.py works but requires manual sys.path manipulation and token lookup. It should be a proper pip-installable package.

Proposed structure:
  ohm/              # package
    client.py        # OHMClient class (current ohm_client.py)
    config.py        # Auto-discover config from /root/olympus/shared/ohm-config.json
    __init__.py      # from ohm.client import OHMClient

Usage would become:
  from ohm.client import OHMClient
  g = OHMClient(actor='metis')  # token auto-loaded from config

This mirrors how the SDK is supposed to work (from ohm.sdk import connect_remote) but uses the HTTP transport that actually functions.

The ohm-config.json already has all agent tokens. The client should read from it automatically.

title: FEATURE: Deduplicate agent registration — multiple nodes per agent
description:
When /register is called multiple times for the same agent (e.g., during integration testing), it creates duplicate agent nodes. The graph currently has 3 'metis' nodes and 2+ nodes for other agents from repeated registrations.

/register should be idempotent: if an agent with the same name already exists, update its values/goals/skills/edges rather than creating a new node.

Implementation:
1. Check if an agent node with label matching the 'name' field exists
2. If yes, update its properties and recreate edges (delete old VALUES/GOALS/CAPABLE_OF edges, create new ones)
3. If no, create as before

This also affects the agent node ID generation — currently generate_node_id('metis') produces different IDs each time because of the hash suffix. Consider using a deterministic ID like 'agent_metis' for registered agents.

title: FEATURE: Agent heartbeat auto-registration in /stats active_agents
description:
GET /stats returns active_agents: 0 even when multiple agents have recently written to the graph. The active_agents count should reflect agents that have called /register or /heartbeat or /state in the last N hours (configurable, default 6).

Currently, active_agents is calculated from ohm_agent_state entries, but no agent calls update_state() during normal operation. Two fixes:

Option A (recommended): Count distinct agent_name values from ohm_change_feed in the last N hours:
  SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT agent_name) FROM ohm_change_feed WHERE occurred_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '6 hours'

Option B: Auto-populate agent_state when /register or /heartbeat is called, and count from that table.

Option A is simpler and doesn't require agents to change their behavior. Option B is more explicit but requires all agents to call update_state().

Found by Deepthought during integration testing. OHM-8gu tracks the symptom; this bead tracks the proper fix.

title: BUG: active_agents always shows 0 in /stats
description:
GET /stats returns active_agents: 0 even when multiple agents have recently written to the graph. The active_agents field should reflect agents that have written in the last N hours or have agent_state entries.

Current: active_agents: 0
Expected: active_agents: 7+ (or count of agents with recent writes in change feed)

Found by Deepthought during integration testing 2026-05-18.

title: FEATURE: Enriched change feed — include node content in listen() response
description:
Current behavior: GET /listen returns raw DB change log entries with table_name, row_id, operation, agent_name, occurred_at. To make sense of a change, agents must do a second GET /node/{row_id} for every entry.

Use case: Deepthought's news workflow needs an activity feed showing what other agents are writing — not just 'ohm_nodes INSERT abc123' but 'metis created a concept node AND→OR Conversion with content: Direction determines function, not operator...'.

Proposed change:
The /listen endpoint should include node content in each entry. Two options:

Option A — Inline enrichment (recommended):
  GET /listen?since=...&enrich=true
  
  Returns entries with an additional 'data' field:
  {
    'id': 196,
    'table_name': 'ohm_nodes',
    'row_id': 'andor_conversion_2a2e17',
    'operation': 'INSERT',
    'agent_name': 'metis',
    'occurred_at': '2026-05-18T15:32:19',
    'data': {
      'label': 'AND→OR Conversion',
      'type': 'pattern',
      'content': 'Direction determines function...',
      'created_by': 'metis'
    }
  }

Option B — Lightweight enrichment:
  Add 'label' and 'type' fields to the change feed table itself (denormalized for fast reads).

Implementation notes:
- Option A requires a JOIN on change feed write or a separate lookup on read
- Option B requires schema migration to add label/type to ohm_change_feed
- Option A is simpler but slower on read; Option B is faster but wider table
- Either way, this is a usability blocker for cross-agent awareness
acceptance_criteria:
1. /listen?enrich=true returns entries with node content for ohm_nodes changes
2. /listen (without enrich) continues to work as before (backward compatible)
3. Edge changes include from_node, to_node, edge_type in data field
4. Performance: <100ms for 100 enriched entries
notes: Implemented Option A: /listen?enrich=true fetches node/edge data on read. Node entries include label, type, content, created_by. Edge entries include from_node, to_node, edge_type, layer.

title: FEATURE: External URL field on nodes
description:
Current behavior: Nodes have 'content' (text) but no native URL field. For news sources, research papers, and external references, there's no way to link a node to its canonical source URL.

Use case: Deepthought creates a 'Reuters Hormuz Report' source node. The content field holds a summary, but there's no structured way to store 'https://reuters.com/article/...'. Same for research papers, GitHub issues, or any external reference.

Proposed schema change:
  ALTER TABLE ohm_nodes ADD COLUMN url TEXT;
  
  POST /node body:
  {id: 'reuters_hormuz', label: 'Reuters Hormuz', type: 'source', content: 'Summary...', url: 'https://reuters.com/...'}

Implementation notes:
- Schema migration to v0.9.0 (batch with other schema changes)
- Backward compatible: nullable column
- store.write_node() accepts and persists url
- GET /node/{id} returns url field
- SDK: create_node(url='...') 
- OHMClient: g.create_node(label, url='https://...')

Verified need: Deepthought integration testing 2026-05-18.
acceptance_criteria:
1. ohm_nodes has url column
2. POST /node accepts and persists url field
3. GET /node/{id} returns url in response
4. Backward compatible: existing nodes without url still work
5. SDK and OHMClient support url parameter

title: FEATURE: Structured source attribution on observations
description:
Current behavior: Observations have a free-text 'notes' field (added in v0.8.0) but no structured fields for source attribution. When Deepthought records an observation like 'Reuters reports ceasefire rejected', the source information is trapped in unstructured text.

Use case: Journalism requires tracking where each observation came from. 'Source: Reuters, URL: https://..., confidence: 0.85' should be typed fields, not parsed from notes.

Proposed schema change:
  ALTER TABLE ohm_observations ADD COLUMN source_url TEXT;
  ALTER TABLE ohm_observations ADD COLUMN source_name TEXT;
  
  The existing 'source' column (TEXT) is vaguely named and already used for observation type ('analysis', 'measurement'). Rename or repurpose:
  - Option A: Keep 'source' as type, add 'source_name' and 'source_url' for attribution
  - Option B: Rename 'source' to 'obs_type', add 'source' (name) and 'source_url'

Proposed API:
  POST /observe/{id}
  Body: {value: 1.5, sigma: 0.5, source_name: 'Reuters', source_url: 'https://...', notes: 'Initial report'}

Implementation notes:
- Schema migration to v0.9.0
- Backward compatible: new columns nullable
- SDK: observe() already accepts source, extend with source_name/source_url
- OHMClient: g.observe(id, value=X, sigma=Y, source_name='Reuters', source_url='https://...')

Verified need: Deepthought integration testing 2026-05-18.
acceptance_criteria:
1. ohm_observations has source_name and source_url columns
2. POST /observe accepts and persists source_name and source_url
3. GET /node/{id} shows observations with source attribution
4. Backward compatible: existing observations still work without new fields
5. SDK and OHMClient updated with new parameters

title: FEATURE: Batch node and edge creation endpoint
description:
Current behavior: POST /node and POST /edge create one item per request. Creating 20 nodes requires 20 HTTP round-trips.

Use case: Deepthought's news workflow creates dozens of nodes per story (event, sources, claims, entities). Batch creation would reduce latency from N×RTT to 1×RTT and allow atomic multi-node writes.

Proposed API:
  POST /batch
  Body:
    {
      "nodes": [
        {"id": "event_1", "label": "Story", "type": "event", ...},
        {"id": "source_1", "label": "Reuters", "type": "source", ...}
      ],
      "edges": [
        {"from": "source_1", "to": "event_1", "type": "REFERENCES", "layer": "L2", ...}
      ]
    }

Response:
  {
    "nodes_created": 2,
    "edges_created": 1,
    "errors": [],
    "change_feed_entries": [id1, id2, id3]
  }

Implementation notes:
- All writes in a single DuckDB transaction for atomicity
- If any node/edge fails validation, roll back entire batch (or partial with errors array)
- Change feed gets one entry per item, not one per batch
- SDK: g.create_batch(nodes=[...], edges=[...])

Verified need: Deepthought integration testing 2026-05-18.
acceptance_criteria:
1. POST /batch accepts nodes[] and edges[] arrays
2. All-or-nothing transaction: either all succeed or none persist
3. Each item populates change feed individually
4. Returns count of created items and any validation errors
5. SDK: OHMClient.create_batch() method

title: FEATURE: Full-text search across label AND content fields
description:
Current behavior: GET /search?q=<term> only matches against node labels. Content field is not searched.

Use case: Deepthought tracks news stories with rich content ('Iran rejected AND-gate ceasefire proposal'). Searching 'ceasefire' returns nothing if no node has 'ceasefire' in its label. For journalism, research, and pattern discovery, content is often where the key information lives.

Proposed fix:
- Extend /search to also match content field using DuckDB LIKE or full-text search
- Add optional ?fields=label,content parameter to control which fields are searched (default: both)
- Maintain backward compatibility — label-only results still included

Example:
  GET /search?q=ceasefire → matches 'Hormuz Ceasefire Collapse' (label) AND 'Iran rejected AND-gate proposal' (content)
  GET /search?q=ceasefire&fields=label → label-only (current behavior)

Implementation notes:
- DuckDB supports LIKE/ILIKE for basic text matching
- For better relevance, consider DuckDB's full-text search extension (pragmatics_fts)
- Index on content may be needed for performance at scale

Verified need: Deepthought integration testing 2026-05-18.
acceptance_criteria:
1. GET /search?q=<term> returns nodes matching in label OR content
2. GET /search?q=<term>&fields=label returns label-only matches (backward compatible)
3. Content matches are clearly indicated in results
4. Performance: <50ms for typical queries on 1000+ node graph

title: FEATURE: Journalism/news workflow enhancements for OHM
description:
Enhancements needed for news/journalism use case (Deepthought):

1. Full-text search across label AND content — search() only matches labels. Need: find all concept nodes containing 'tariffs' in either label or content field.

2. Batch node creation — g.create_nodes([...]) for bulk operations. News workflow = dozens of nodes per story.

3. Structured source attribution on observations — current notes field is unstructured text. Need: source, url, article_url as typed fields on observations.

4. External URL field on nodes — nodes have content (text) but no native url or source_url field. Each story node should have a canonical source link.

5. Label substring filtering on list_nodes — can filter by node_type but not by label_contains. Need: list_nodes(node_type='concept', label_contains='china')

Found during Deepthought integration testing 2026-05-18.
notes: Also: list_nodes returns {nodes:[], total:N} dict while search returns raw list — inconsistent. Fixed in ohm_client.py wrapper.
close_reason: Split into individual feature beads: OHM-s8q, OHM-b9v, OHM-k3j, OHM-w4m, OHM-t7n

title: BUG: Observation notes parameter accepted but not persisted or returned
description:
POST /observe/{id} accepts a 'notes' field in the request body but the notes are not returned in the response. Unclear whether they're persisted at all.

The schema (ohm_observations) may not have a notes column, or the write/read paths skip it.

Found by Hephaestus during OHM integration testing.

title: BUG: DELETE /node with invalid ID returns 500 instead of validation error
description:
When DELETE /node/{id} receives an ID containing spaces or special characters (like a label instead of an ID), the daemon returns 500 Internal Server Error instead of a proper 400 validation error. The validate_identifier() function is not called on the DELETE path.

Found by Hephaestus during OHM integration testing. Worked around in ohm_client.py with _resolve_node_id() but the daemon should validate input.

title: FEATURE: No node search/list endpoint — agents cannot browse or discover existing nodes
description:
There is no GET /nodes endpoint to list or search existing nodes without knowing their exact ID. The SDK's query() method relies on SQL against a local DuckDB, which doesn't work with the daemon (see OHM-776).

Agents need a way to discover what's in the graph. Options:
1. GET /nodes with pagination and type/label filtering
2. GET /nodes/search?q=... for text search
3. Ensure SDK query() works through HTTP API

Without this, agents must know exact node IDs to interact with the graph.

Verified by Metis during OHM integration testing 2026-05-18.

title: BUG: SDK field names don't match HTTP API — create_edge uses from_node/to_node but API expects from/to
description:
SDK's create_edge() passes from_node, to_node, edge_type parameters. The HTTP API expects from, to, type. This means any agent using the SDK to create edges via the REST API would get validation errors.

Similarly, create_node() auto-generates IDs from labels, but the HTTP API requires an explicit id field.

Field mapping gaps:
- SDK: from_node → HTTP: from
- SDK: to_node → HTTP: to  
- SDK: edge_type → HTTP: type
- SDK: auto-generated ID → HTTP: requires explicit id

Verified by Metis during OHM integration testing 2026-05-18.

title: BUG: No DELETE endpoint — agents cannot remove test or erroneous data
description:
DELETE /node/{id} and DELETE /edge/{id} return 501 Not Implemented. Agents cannot remove incorrect or test data from the graph. Over time this creates pollution.

The daemon logs show: 'code 501, message Unsupported method (DELETE)' for all DELETE requests.

Verified by Metis during OHM integration testing 2026-05-18.

title: Docs: add ADR for observation type extensibility (sentiment, vibration, etc.)
description:
Current observation types (anomaly, measurement, pattern, challenge, support) are too narrow for the new scenarios:

Medical: 'diagnostic', 'vital_sign', 'lab_result'
Cybersecurity: 'alert', 'indicator', 'log_entry'
Supply chain: 'disruption', 'shipment', 'inventory_level'
Customer support: 'sentiment', 'resolution', 'satisfaction'

Current VALID_OBSERVATION_TYPES is a frozenset — adding types requires schema.py changes. Need an ADR on how to make observation types extensible:

Option A: Validated frozenset with domain configs (like edge types in SchemaConfig)
Option B: Open string with suggested types (lenient validation)
Option C: Per-deployment config (ohmd.json defines valid types)

This also affects VALID_OBSERVATION_SOURCES — medical needs 'lab', 'imaging'; cybersecurity needs 'siem', 'edr'.

Decision needed before OHM-af8 scenario implementations.

title: No SIGPIPE handling in daemon — crash on broken connections
description:
run_server in server.py only handles SIGTERM and SIGINT. No SIGPIPE handling. Python's http.server can crash on broken pipe when client disconnects mid-response. Add signal.signal(signal.SIGPIPE, signal.SIG_IGN) to prevent crashes.

Found by: Atlas OHM Review subagent

title: docs: domain edge type guide — when to use what (OHM-m2b.7)
description:
Agents need guidance on which edge type to use for domain-specific relationships. Create docs/edge-type-guide.md as a reference for domain designers.

Sections:
1. Core edge types — what each of the existing 30+ edge types means and when to use
2. Domain extension pattern — adding new edge types via SchemaConfig (ADR-007)
3. Cattle edge types — PASTURE_FEEDS, ROTATION_SCHEDULES, HERD_AFFECTED_BY with examples
4. Retail edge types — BATCH_EXPIRES_BEFORE, COMPETITOR_RUNS_SALE, EMPLOYEE_COVERED_BY
5. Decision tree: I

title: SDK: batch expiry detection helper — g.expiring_soon(product_type, days=5) (OHM-m2b.6)
description:
In the retail scenario, the inventory agent needs to track delivery batches and alert when they approach expiry. Currently this requires manual query construction.

New SDK method:


Returns batches sorted by expiry date ascending, with days_until_expiry computed.

Also add BATCH_EXPIRES_BEFORE edge type to the retail SchemaConfig and to LAYER_EDGE_TYPES['L2'].

Implementation:
1. Add BATCH_EXPIRES_BEFORE to LAYER_EDGE_TYPES['L2'] in schema.py
2. Add expiring_soon() to SDK (wraps query_find_expiring_batches)
3. Add query in queries/__init__.py
4. Test with mock expiry edges
5. Document in retail-scenario.md

Acceptance criteria:
- Returns batches expiring within the given days window
- Sorted by expiry date (soonest first)
- Optional product_type filter works
- days_until_expiry field computed correctly

title: ohmd: SSE /events filter by node_type and node_id (OHM-m2b.5)
description:
The /events SSE endpoint currently filters by layer and agent. The inventory agent in the retail scenario only wants to hear about DELIVERY_BATCH nodes and EMPLOYEE nodes — not every node in the graph.

Add node_type and node_id filters to SSE /events:
- ?node_type=DELIVERY_BATCH,EMPLOYEE — only changes to these node types
- ?node_id=batch-123 — only changes to this specific node
- Combines with existing ?layer and ?agent filters

Implementation:
1. Add node_type and node_id query params to /events handler
2. Pass through to query_build_change_feed (or new filter function)
3. Add tests for node_type filter, node_id filter, combined filters
4. Update /openapi.json schema

Acceptance criteria:
- node_type filter returns only events for matching node types
- Multiple node_types can be specified (comma-separated)
- node_id filter returns only events for that specific node
- Filters compose with layer and agent filters

title: SDK: temporal confidence decay for time-sensitive observations (OHM-m2b.4)
description:
Observations in OHM have static confidence. But in retail, weather at 2pm is very different from weather at 4pm. Temperature observations should decay in confidence as they age. composite_score() uses raw observation values without temporal weighting — a 94°F reading from 8am and one from 4pm contribute equally.

This is particularly critical for composite_score with the geometric method (OHM-0e0.3): if temperature observations compound multiplicatively over a day, a stale 8am reading can distort the entire day's demand calculation.

**Two complementary fixes:**

1. Add temporal_decay parameter to composite_score():


When temporal_decay_hours is set, observation values are weighted by exp(-age_hours / temporal_decay_hours). Retail: temporal_decay_hours=4.0 (weather relevant for 4 hours). Cattle: temporal_decay_hours=168.0 (7 days for NDVI).

2. Add standalone decay_observations() as a substrate method:


This updates observation values in-place with time-decayed versions (or returns what would change if dry_run=True).

**Implementation approach:**
- Option A: observations are immutable log — decay is always computed at read time (in composite_score). No storage change.
- Option B: observations are mutable — decay_observations() writes decayed values back.
- Recommendation: Option A. Observations are a log. Temporal weighting is a query-time concern. The decay formula: effective_weight = 0.5^(age_hours / half_life_hours).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- composite_score with temporal_decay_hours=4.0 weights 8am observation at ~25%% by 4pm (2 half-lives)
- decay_observations(dry_run=True) shows what would change without modifying
- decay_observations() with node_id=None decays all observations
- Works alongside apply_decay() for edges — both can run independently
- Test: retail weather observation (4hr half-life) vs cattle NDVI observation (168hr half-life)

title: /stats HTTP endpoint — already works in CLI, surface to HTTP
SSE layer/agent filter — ?layer=L3&agent=vegetation on /events
SDK: CONTRADICTS edge type and auto-population on opposing L3 assertions
description: OHM-1g3.2 adds contradictions() query, but a more powerful pattern is auto-creating CONTRADICTS edges when OHM detects opposing assertions about the same subject. When Vegetation Agent and Herd Behavior Agent both create L3 nodes about the same pasture with opposite conclusions, OHM should automatically create a CONTRICTS edge between them. Implementation: (1) add CONTRADICTS as an edge type in schema (L2 layer), (2) in write_observation and create_node, query for existing L3 nodes about the same subject with different conclusions, (3) auto-create CONTRADICTS edge with metadata {node_a, node_b, detected_at, contradiction_type}. This turns contradiction detection from pull (query) to push (subscription via change feed).
acceptance_criteria: When two L3 nodes about the same pasture with opposing conclusions are created, a CONTRADICTS edge is auto-created between them; CONTRADICTS edges visible in neighborhood queries; detect_trend edge type added to schema; edge metadata includes contradiction_type and detected_at timestamp

title: ADR-007: Schema evolution and type governance for domain expansion
description: The cattle scenario introduces new domain types (CALVING_EVENT, HOOF_SCORE, PARASITE_LOAD, ANIMAL_GROUP) that didn't exist in the original schema. OHM currently has no schema migration story — node_types are advisory. Write ADR-007 that covers: (1) Schema versioning — how to add new node/edge types without breaking existing queries, (2) Type registry — a canonical list of valid types with descriptions, owned by domain, (3) Migration path — how to promote an experimental node_type to canonical, (4) Validation policy — advisory vs. enforced schema, (5) Domain separation — how cattle domain types don't pollute the core OHM ontology. Reference ADR-001, ADR-005 for patterns.
acceptance_criteria: ADR-007 written in docs/adr/; covers versioning, type registry, migration, validation policy; references ADR-001, ADR-005; reviewed and merged

title: SDK: g.detect_trend(node_id, metric, window_days) — temporal pattern detection from observation history
description: NDVI declining over 60 days is a temporal pattern, not a single observation. Currently detecting it requires: query all observations at T1 and T2, compute delta, store derived node. Every agent re-implements this. Add g.detect_trend(node_id, window_days=30, metric='value') that: (1) queries all observations of the given node within the window, (2) fits a trend line (simple: first-last delta; robust: linear regression), (3) returns {direction: 'improving'|'stable'|'degrading', delta_per_day, confidence, data_points, regression_r2}, (4) optionally creates a derived L3 TREND node with the result. This is needed by Vegetation Agent (NDVI), Welfare Agent (welfare scores), Infrastructure Agent (water levels). Pattern: every temporal metric needs this.
acceptance_criteria: g.detect_trend('river-bottom-pasture', window_days=60) returns {direction, delta_per_day, confidence, data_points}; works on any node with observations; linear regression with r2; creates L3 TREND node if requested

title: ADR-006: Formalize agent discoverability contract
description: ADR-005 established that agents call ohm, not raw SQL, but didn't specify HOW agents discover what ohm can do. Write ADR-006 that codifies: (1) Every surface (CLI, SDK, HTTP) must be self-documenting at runtime, (2) Discovery endpoints/methods return structured metadata, (3) No agent should need to read source code to use the system, (4) Schema and capability info is always available without auth. This turns the pattern from ad-hoc into contract.
acceptance_criteria: ADR-006 written in docs/adr/; references ADR-005; covers CLI, SDK, HTTP requirements; defines discoverability contract; reviewed and merged

title: ohmd: Add OpenAPI 3.0 spec endpoint at /openapi.json
description: While GET / (OHM-azn.2) provides a human-friendly route index, machine clients benefit from a formal OpenAPI spec. Add GET /openapi.json that returns a valid OpenAPI 3.0 document describing all endpoints, parameters, request bodies, and response schemas. This enables standard tooling (Swagger UI, code generation, validation) and makes ohmd discoverable by any OpenAPI-aware agent. Auto-generate from route definitions in server.py.
acceptance_criteria: GET /openapi.json returns valid OpenAPI 3.0 JSON; covers all endpoints with params and schemas; passes openapi-schema-validator; auto-generated from server.py routes

title: SDK: Bridge CLI↔SDK parity gap — decay, query, schema, status, upgrade
description: Several CLI commands have no SDK equivalent: decay, query (freeform), schema, status, upgrade. An agent using the SDK must shell out to the CLI for these, which violates ADR-005. Specifically: graph.apply_decay() for confidence decay, graph.query() for structured/freeform queries, graph.schema()/graph.layers() (covered by OHM-azn.1), graph.status() for daemon state. The queries/ module already has most of these — they just need SDK wrapping.
acceptance_criteria: graph.apply_decay() wraps apply_confidence_decay; graph.query() wraps structured queries; graph.status() returns daemon/graph status; graph.upgrade() handles schema migrations; no CLI-only functionality remains

title: SDK: Add graph.help() introspection method
description: An agent importing ohm.sdk cannot discover available Graph methods at runtime. Add graph.help() that returns structured metadata about every public method: name, signature, docstring, parameters, return type. This lets an agent learn the API by asking the system instead of reading source code. Pattern: Python inspect module on Graph class, filtered to public non-dunder methods.
acceptance_criteria: graph.help() returns list of {name, signature, docstring, params, return_type}; covers all public Graph methods; importable without prior knowledge of the API

title: TestIssue123
Add confidence decay — auto-decay edge confidence over time (30-day half-life)
Add graph visualization — Mermaid/Graphviz export for ohm graph commands
Add observability — structured logging, metrics endpoint, request tracing
Add WebSocket/SSE push notifications — stop polling listen() for change feed
Fix 22 E501 line-too-long violations across codebase
Webhook push notifications — stop polling listen()
description: listen() is polling only. For production, agents need push notifications when relevant changes occur. Options: (1) webhook callback URL registered per agent, (2) WebSocket long-lived connection, (3) server-sent events. Recommendation: webhooks first (simplest). Agent registers callback URL, substrate POSTs change events matching INTERESTED_IN edges.

title: Substrate method: Bayesian confidence aggregation
description: Combine multiple observations on a node using Bayesian fusion. Input: list of (value, confidence) observations. Output: aggregated value, combined confidence, observation count. Same result regardless of which agent calls it — that's why it's substrate. Implementation: conjugate prior update (beta-normal for continuous values). See docs/substrate-methods.md.
close_reason: Implemented in methods.py. aggregate_observations() with weighted (inverse-variance), mean, max_confidence, consensus strategies. SDK: aggregate(node_id, method='weighted'). Substrate method.

title: Substrate method: Monte Carlo impact simulation
description: Given a node, simulate downstream impact using Monte Carlo sampling. Randomly enable/disable edges based on confidence, propagate through graph N times. Output: probability of reaching each downstream node, expected impact magnitude. Same result regardless of caller. See docs/substrate-methods.md method 2.
close_reason: Implemented. monte_carlo_impact() in methods.py. Monte Carlo simulation samples edge activation based on confidence and traces downstream impact. SDK: monte_carlo(node_id, simulations=1000, depth=3). HTTP: /monte-carlo/NODE_ID?simulations=1000&depth=3. Substrate method — same result regardless of caller. Tested: Hormuz → 94.9% oil spike → 75.4% recession → 44.6% rate cuts.

title: Substrate method: Anomaly detection by sigma threshold
description: Find observations where (value - baseline) / sigma > threshold. Already designed in observation schema (sigma field). Implementation: simple SQL query, no ML needed. Returns: list of anomalous observations with deviation magnitude. Same result regardless of caller.
close_reason: Implemented in methods.py. detect_anomalies() flags observations where |value-baseline|/sigma > threshold. SDK: anomalies(sigma_threshold=2.0). Substrate method.

title: Connection discovery — suggest links between unconnected nodes
description: Identify unlinked nodes, suggest connections, flag dense clusters. Similar to zettelkasten discover.py suggest but powered by graph structure. (1) Orphan detection: nodes with 0 edges. (2) Suggest: nodes with shared tags/topics but no direct edge. (3) Cluster: 3+ nodes densely connected but no synthesis. Output: suggestions with confidence. Agent decides whether to create the edge.
close_reason: Implemented. suggest_connections() SDK method. Two strategies: (1) shared tags — nodes with overlapping tag sets but no existing edge, (2) co-occurrence — nodes that share neighbors but aren't directly connected. Score-normalized. Substrate method.

title: Confidence calibration — track agent accuracy over time
description: Track which agents tend to be overconfident or underconfident. If Hephaestus consistently writes conf: 0.9 on findings that get challenged, the substrate notes calibration drift. Implementation: compare declared confidence vs support/challenge ratio over time. Output: per-agent calibration factor. Used by attention routing and aggregation. See docs/agent-relationship-challenges.md meta-challenge.
close_reason: Implemented. compute_confidence_calibration() in methods.py. Tracks challenge rate by confidence band for L3/L4 edges. Calibration score 0-1 (1=perfect). SDK: calibration(agent_name). HTTP: /calibration/AGENT_NAME. Substrate method.

title: Deduplication — recognize when agents observe the same thing
description: When 3 agents observe the same event independently, the substrate should recognize they're talking about the same thing and link the observations. Not merge — link. 'Three agents observed X, here are their independent assessments.' Mechanism: label similarity (fuzzy match) + shared topic edges + temporal proximity. Threshold: similarity > 0.8. Creates RELATED_TO edge between observations.
close_reason: Implemented. detect_near_duplicates() in methods.py. Flags observations from different agents on same node, same type, values within similarity threshold, created within 1 hour. SDK: near_duplicates(). HTTP: /duplicates?similarity=0.8. Substrate method. Tested: Metis 0.85 vs Clio 0.87 = 97.7% similarity.

title: Edge versioning — history when agent updates own edges
description: When an agent updates their own edge (update_edge), the old value is overwritten. No history. For L3/L4 edges, we should track what changed. Options: (1) change feed already logs old_data/new_data — query that, (2) append-only edge_versions table, (3) never update — always create new edge and deprecate old. Recommendation: option 1 first (change feed already has it), option 3 long-term (consistent with challenge semantics — don't modify, create new).
close_reason: Implemented. edge_history() SDK method. Full lifecycle tracking: creation, confidence updates, challenges, supports, identity evolution (superseded/evolved_to chain). Reads from change feed + reactions + metadata. Chronological order. 4 tests.

title: Graph import/export — JSON backup and restore
description: No way to export or import graph data. Need: ohm export (full graph as JSON), ohm import (restore from JSON), ohm merge (import without overwriting). Critical for backup, migration, and testing (seed graph with test data). Format: {nodes: [...], edges: [...], observations: [...], agent_configs: [...]}.
close_reason: Implemented. export_graph() + import_graph() SDK methods. Full round-trip: nodes, edges, observations, agent_state. Merge mode skips duplicates. Replace mode clears first. Meta includes schema version and export timestamp. JSON-compatible dict.

title: ohmd encrypted token storage
description: Tokens in plaintext. Hash for verification, secrets.compare_digest for constant-time compare.

title: Substrate services — memory, connections, attention, decay, provenance, deduplication
description:
The substrate should handle everything every agent needs but none should build alone.

10 services:
1. Memory (P0, done) — substrate IS the memory
2. Attention routing (P0, needs agent registration) — change feed with relevance
3. Provenance tracking (P1) — causal chains: observation → interpretation → challenge → synthesis
4. Decay and staleness (P1) — confidence auto-decays, 30-day half-life
5. Contradiction detection (P1) — flag conflicting observations/challenges
6. Connection discovery (P1) — suggest links, find orphans, detect clusters
7. Context retrieval (P2) — relevance scoring, graph-proximity-based
8. Deduplication (P2) — link independent observations of same event
9. Confidence calibration (P2) — track agent over/underconfidence by domain
10. Synthesis prompts (P2) — prompt agents when clusters reach critical mass

Line: substrate does pattern matching and bookkeeping. Agents do meaning-making.

See docs/substrate-services.md for full design.
close_reason: Design complete: docs/substrate-services.md. 10 substrate services defined with priority/dependencies. Implementation blocked on P0/P1 completion.

title: Substrate methods — validated computation in the cognition substrate
description:
OHM isn't just a knowledge graph — it's a cognition substrate. When a computation produces the same result regardless of which agent calls it, it belongs in the substrate, not in each agent's ad-hoc implementation.

Substrate-owned methods:
1. Confidence aggregation (bayesian, inverse-variance, mean, consensus) — 'ohm graph aggregate'
2. Monte Carlo impact analysis — 'ohm graph impact --method monte-carlo'
3. Anomaly detection (sigma-based) — 'ohm graph anomalies'
4. Change feed with relevance scoring — 'ohm graph listen --min-relevance'
5. Graph health metrics — 'ohm graph health'

Agent-owned (stays with agents):
- Research methodology, challenge strategy, synthesis judgment, trading signals

Implementation: each method in ohm/methods/ with pure function, SDK wrapper, CLI command, test suite.

See docs/substrate-methods.md for full design.

Dependencies:
- OHM-a35.10 (agent registration) needed for relevance scoring
- OHM-a35.9 (SDK read methods) needed for aggregate/anomaly
- Monte Carlo needs confidence propagation in impact analysis
close_reason: Design complete: docs/substrate-methods.md. 5 substrate methods defined. Implementation blocked on P0/P1 completion.

title: Confidence aggregation — how to combine multiple agent observations
description:
VISION.md says 'accumulate, don't collapse' — three observations on the same node stay as three separate rows. Downstream consumers choose how to aggregate.

But the graph doesn't help them. When I query a node's observations, I get three rows with different values and confidences. There's no guidance on how to combine them.

Design question: Should OHM provide aggregation functions, or leave it entirely to the consumer?

Options:
1. Pure accumulate — store only, consumer aggregates (current design)
2. Provide aggregation hints — add aggregation_strategy to node metadata ('mean', 'max', 'bayesian', 'weighted')
3. Compute derived confidence — when 3 agents observe the same thing, compute a combined confidence score using Bayesian updating or inverse-variance weighting, store it as a separate L2 edge

Option 1 is cleanest but pushes complexity to every consumer. Option 3 is most useful but requires defining the aggregation function.

For now: keep Option 1 (pure accumulate). But add a note in the schema docs that this is an open design question, and add observation count + mean/min/max confidence to the stats endpoint so consumers can make informed decisions without querying every observation.

Acceptance:
- stats endpoint includes observation_count and confidence distribution per node
- ADR or docs note that aggregation strategy is an open design question
- No changes to the accumulation model (keep current behavior)
close_reason: Implemented in 5cd151c. Observation stats (count, mean, min, max confidence) added to /stats endpoint.

title: Change feed consumer — push notifications for agents
description:
ohm graph listen exists but agents can only poll for changes. For a 'shared awareness' system, push notifications matter — agents should be able to subscribe to changes in their domain.

Current: agent calls listen() periodically, gets changes since last call.
Needed: agent subscribes to change types/layers, gets notified when relevant changes happen.

Options:
1. WebSocket in ohmd — agents connect, subscribe to layers/edge_types, get push updates
2. Webhook — agent registers a URL, ohmd POSTs changes
3. Polling with long-poll — listen?since=<last>&timeout=30 blocks until new changes
4. Event queue — each agent has a queue, changes are pushed to relevant queues

Option 3 (long-poll) is simplest and doesn't require WebSocket infrastructure. Option 1 is best for real-time.

Acceptance:
- Agents can subscribe to specific layers and edge types
- Changes are delivered within seconds, not minutes
- Backpressure: if agent falls behind, changes queue up to a limit then oldest are dropped
- Change feed includes: who made the change, what changed, old and new values
close_reason: Implemented. listen() SDK method — change feed consumer with topic/agent/operation filtering. Excludes own changes by default. Updates last_sync on read. pending_notifications() shortcut. 4 new tests.

title: Schema migration strategy — version field and upgrade tool
description:
No schema migration story exists. What happens when the schema changes? There's no version field on the database, no migration tool, no 'ohm upgrade' command.

DuckDB makes schema changes easier than Postgres (ALTER TABLE works, ADD COLUMN is cheap), but agents need to know what version they're talking to and the daemon needs to apply migrations on startup.

Design:
- Add schema_version to a metadata table (ohm_meta or similar)
- On startup, compare current schema version with code version
- Apply migrations incrementally (1→2, 2→3, etc.)
- Each migration is a named SQL script in a migrations/ directory
- 'ohm graph status' shows current schema version

Acceptance:
- Schema version tracked in database
- Migrations applied automatically on startup
- 'ohm upgrade' command for manual migration
- No data loss on migration
close_reason: Implemented in ef8c123 + ff0351f. Schema migration framework with version tracking in ohm_meta, upgrade command, migration scripts.

title: pytest-benchmark not installed: 8 benchmark tests error
description: 8 benchmark tests in test_benchmarks.py use the pytest-benchmark fixture, but pytest-benchmark was not installed under the active Python (3.13). The pyproject.toml already lists pytest-benchmark>=4.0 in [dev] deps — the issue was a Python version mismatch (deps installed under 3.12, pytest running under 3.13). Fixed by running: python -m pip install -e '.[dev]'. All 8 benchmark tests now pass. RESOLVED: dependency was already declared, just needed reinstall under correct Python.

title: Remove dead code: query.py NLP parser
description:
src/ohm/query.py (126 lines) is a natural language query parser that is never used by any production code. The CLI's 'graph query' command uses structured filtering, not NLP. The parser is only imported by test_ohm.py and test_integration.py for unit testing, but nothing in src/ actually calls it.

Decision: Remove it for now. If we want NLP query later, it belongs in the SDK as a higher-level feature, not as a standalone module.

Acceptance:
- File removed
- Tests for parse_query removed or migrated
- 144 tests still pass (minus the removed parse_query tests)
close_reason: Implemented in 2567515. query.py (NLP parser) removed — never used by production code.

title: Performance benchmarks — CTE query latency, concurrent throughput
description: Benchmark suite: (1) neighborhood query latency at scale (1K, 10K, 100K nodes), (2) path finding latency, (3) impact analysis latency, (4) concurrent read/write throughput via Quack. Store results in docs/benchmarks/.
notes: Benchmark suite added in 89e2848 but requires pytest-benchmark (not in dev deps). 8 tests error on missing 'benchmark' fixture. Tests pass with -k 'not benchmark'.

title: TOPO schema — industrial node/edge types, layer customization
description: Extend OHM schema with TOPO-specific types: equipment, system, area, site nodes; FEEDS, FLOWS_TO, DEPENDS_ON edges. Allow layer customization (e.g., L4 in industrial context = predictive maintenance). Make schema configurable so OHM and TOPO share the same engine with different configs.

title: TOPO CLI — industrial-specific commands
description: Add 'topo' CLI entry point with domain-specific commands: topo failure-analysis <equipment-id>, topo compliance-map <system-id>, topo impact-study <change-id>. These wrap OHM graph queries with industrial semantics.

title: Shared daemon codebase — ohmd/topod from same source
description: Factor ohmd so that schema, edge types, and node types are configurable. Create 'topod' as an entry point that loads the TOPO schema instead of the default. One codebase, two instantiations.

title: P2: TOPO — Industrial Knowledge Graph
description: TOPO is OHM instantiated for industrial knowledge. Same architecture (L1-L4, challenge semantics, agent attribution) but domain-specific node types (equipment, system, area, site) and edge types (FEEDS, FLOWS_TO, DEPENDS_ON). Shared daemon codebase with different schema configuration.

title: Performance benchmarks — CTE query latency, concurrent agent throughput
description: Establish performance baselines for OHM. Benchmarks: CTE query latency (neighborhood at depth 1-5, path finding, impact analysis), concurrent agent throughput (1-20 simultaneous agents), write latency (single edge vs batch), sync latency (local cache → DuckLake), snapshot query time at various graph sizes (100-100K edges). **Design decisions needed:** benchmark framework, graph size generation strategy, result storage and visualization, regression detection in CI. **PM input needed:** target performance SLAs (p95 latency for each query type), max supported graph size for v1, concurrent agent target.

title: Shared ohmd/topod daemon codebase — one pattern, two instantiations
description: Refactor ohmd into a shared daemon library that both ohm and topo can use. Extract: Quack server management, DuckDB lifecycle, token auth, health check, graceful shutdown. Both ohmd and topod use the same binary with different configs. **Design decisions needed:** code organization (shared library vs config-driven single binary), daemon naming convention, config file discovery for each instance. **PM input needed:** should ohmd and topod run on the same host or separate hosts? Can they share a DuckLake backend?

title: TOPO CLI — industrial-specific commands on OHM architecture
description: Implement topo CLI as a specialized instantiation of the OHM architecture. Commands: topo graph impact <equipment-id> (failure propagation), topo graph topology <area-id> (physical connectivity), topo graph causal-chain <effect> (root cause analysis), topo observe <sensor-id> --value <v> (ingest telemetry), topo alarm <equipment-id> --type <type> (create alarm node). Reuses ohmd daemon, Quack, DuckLake stack. **Design decisions needed:** CLI code sharing strategy (ohm as library vs fork vs symlink), command naming conventions, output format for industrial users (tables vs diagrams). **PM input needed:** which industrial use case should TOPO target first? What is the expected user profile (engineer, operator, data scientist)?

title: TOPO-specific schema — edge types, node types, layer customization
description: Design TOPO-specific schema extensions on top of OHM base schema. TOPO edge types: FEEDS (material flow), CAUSES (failure propagation), PREDICTS (model output), CONTROLS (actuator/sensor), MONITORS (observation), DEPENDS_ON (operational dependency). TOPO node types: equipment, system, area, site, sensor, actuator, controller. TOPO layer mapping: L1=physical topology, L2=material/energy flow, L3=causal models, L4=forecast/prognosis. **Design decisions needed:** schema extension mechanism (new tables vs type enums vs separate database), TOPO-specific validation rules, cross-domain edge types (OHM↔TOPO). **PM input needed:** which TOPO domains are first priority (manufacturing, energy, transportation)? What is the relationship between OHM and TOPO instances?

title: Phase 5: TOPO Instantiation — Apply OHM architecture to industrial knowledge graph
description: Goal: topo CLI running on same architecture as ohm. One pattern, two instantiations. Ongoing.

title: /ate response uses 'ate' key but some callers expect 'average_treatment_effect'
description: Métis flagged an inconsistency: the /ate endpoint response uses 'ate' as the effect size key, but some callers or documentation may reference it as 'average_treatment_effect'. This causes silent failures in callers that check the wrong key. Need to audit all callers and either standardize the key or add an alias.
close_reason: False alarm — 'ate' key is consistent throughout. compute_ate returns {"ate": float} and all callers (compute_sensitivity, compute_voi) look up ate_result["ate"]. No inconsistency exists.

title: Document cycling-pair ATE attenuation as known limitation
description: The cycle breaker necessarily removes edges to form a valid DAG, which attenuates measured causal effects for cycling pairs. e.g. hormuz<->demand-rationing ATE=0.07. This should be documented as a known limitation of the do-operator on cyclic graphs rather than treated as a bug.

title: /tasks has no PATCH endpoint — cannot update task status
description: Tasks can be created via POST /tasks but there is no PATCH /tasks/<id> to transition status. Agents cannot mark tasks in-progress or complete them via the API.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 44a4d92 — added PATCH /tasks/ route as alias for PATCH /node/ in server.py

title: Personal/college nodes mislabeled as L3 geopolitical layer
description: Nodes like decision-college-2026, path-hollins-interdisciplinary, domain-gaming have CAUSES edges tagged layer=L3. This causes /suggest_causes?layers=L3 to return mixed-domain candidates (geopolitical + personal). The code correctly filters by causal graph membership, but the edges are mis-labeled so these nodes ARE in the L3 causal graph by mistake. Fix: relabel personal domain edges to L2 or an appropriate personal layer.

title: Restructure src/ohm/ into server/, inference/, graph/, framework/ subdirectories
description: src/ohm/ is a flat directory of large files (server.py 2500+ lines, bayesian.py 2700+ lines, store.py, db.py, etc.). As inference gets decoupled (OHM-p2u8) and routing gets structured (OHM-c5pl), the natural module boundaries become: server/ (routing, handlers, auth, middleware), inference/ (bayesian, markov, pert, causal_refutation), graph/ (store, db, schema, queries, migrations), framework/ (GraphReader protocol, SemanticRoles, IngestAdapter, client, sdk). Pure reorganization — no behavior changes. Import paths change (ohm.bayesian → ohm.inference.bayesian) with re-exports from ohm.* for backward compat. Should be done after OHM-p2u8 and OHM-c5pl when module boundaries are stable.
close_reason: Already implemented in the restructure/GraphReader/SemanticRoles/IngestAdapter work from this session.

title: Define IngestAdapter protocol for domain data bridge pattern
description: Every domain app has the same ingestion problem: external data source → OHM graph. Currently each integration is bespoke with different error handling, provenance tagging, and idempotency. IngestAdapter is a Protocol that domain apps implement: read_batch() -> Iterable[IngestRecord] and source_id() -> str. A generic run_ingest(adapter, client, dry_run=False) runner handles writing to OHM, idempotency via node/edge upsert semantics, provenance tagging with adapter.source_id(), and error recovery with per-record logging. TOPO implements TimescaleDBAdapter(IngestAdapter). Depends on GraphReader being clean (OHM-p2u8) so the runner can use the same record types.
close_reason: Already implemented in the restructure/GraphReader/SemanticRoles/IngestAdapter work from this session.

title: Add weibull_to_pert_anchor() to pert.py
description: Domain apps using actuarial failure data (Weibull shape β, scale η parameters from reliability engineering) need to bridge to OHM's PERT-based uncertainty representation. The math is closed-form: Weibull CDF inverse gives P(failure before t) = 1 - exp(-(t/η)^β), so t_p = η * (-ln(1-p))^(1/β). weibull_to_pert_anchor(beta, eta, horizon_days, percentiles=(0.05, 0.50, 0.95)) returns (base_rate, p05, p50, p95) suitable for passing to anchored_pert(). base_rate = P(failure within horizon_days). Output is 'probability of failure within horizon_days', not an annual rate.

title: PERT elicitation automation — populate p05/p50/p95 on edges from agent knowledge
description: AGENT WORK — not a dev-team task. The pert.py module and API endpoints exist. Agents (metis, clio, socrates) populate PERT estimates on edges via POST /edge with probability_p05/p50/p95. Need: (1) Agent prompts for PERT estimates, (2) Bulk PERT update endpoint (already: POST /observations for nodes, need PATCH /edge for PERT fields), (3) Mixture-of-experts solicitation for high-utility_scale decisions.

title: CLEANUP: Fix 57 auto-fixable lint errors
FEATURE: OHM-o9f Semantic search via embeddings + VSS index
description:
Add semantic (vector) search using DuckDB's VSS extension. Currently uses ILIKE which matches tokens, not meaning.

Known working:
- VSS extension is installed and loads in DuckDB 1.5.2
- HNSW index creation works (tested)
- array_cosine_similarity function works
- Ollama API for embeddings accessible at localhost:11434

Architecture:
1. Add embedding column to ohm_nodes (FLOAT[768])
2. Create HNSW index: CREATE INDEX USING HNSW (embedding vector_cosine_distance)
3. GET /semantic_search?q=QUERY: generate embedding via Ollama, find nearest neighbors
4. Optional: hybrid search combining semantic + ILIKE

Activation: 500+ nodes OR when ILIKE search becomes insufficient

Research needed:
- Optimal embedding model for OHM (short text, patterns, concepts)
- Embedding generation latency and batching
- Whether to store embeddings or compute on-demand

Estimated: ~200 lines + embedding pipeline
acceptance_criteria:
1. GET /semantic_search?q=QUERY returns semantically related nodes
2. HNSW index on embedding column
3. Index maintained on node INSERT/UPDATE
4. Semantic results are meaningfully different from ILIKE

title: FEATURE: OHM-gwg SDK unauthenticated reads — security decision needed
description:
DECISION NEEDED: The OHM SDK's OHMClient allows reads (GET /node, /stats, /search, /neighborhood, /listen) WITHOUT a token. The token is only required for writes. This means anyone who can reach the API can read all nodes, edges, observations, and change feed.

Two possible positions:

Position A — Intentional (public read model):
- Agents don't need credentials to read the shared graph
- Token only needed for writes (accountability)
- Mitigation: Firewall to trusted networks only

Position B — Security concern:
- Change feed reveals all write activity (who wrote what, when)
- Node content may contain sensitive observations or patterns
- Agents should authenticate even for reads

Decision needed:
1. Is public-read intentional?
2. If yes: document it, add network-level controls
3. If no: fix SDK to require token for all operations
acceptance_criteria:
1. Decision documented in OHM README: public-read allowed or token required
2. If fix: SDK sends token on all requests, unauthorized reads return 401

title: FEATURE: OHM-8c6 Metis zettelkasten → OHM bridge
description:
Project Metis zettelkasten patterns into the OHM shared graph for cross-agent reasoning. Metis is the knowledge companion; her private Zettelkasten contains synthesized insights, patterns, and connections. This bridge makes those insights accessible to other agents.

Trigger conditions (activate when):
- Graph has 500+ nodes (OHM performance threshold)
- OR manual trigger from Metis ("project to OHM" command)

What gets projected:
1. Pattern notes (labels, content, confidence) → OHM concept/pattern nodes (L3)
2. Wikilinks between notes → OHM edges (REFINES, SUPPORTS, etc.)
3. Tags → node labels or ohm_nodes.tags field
4. Source references → provenance field

What STAYS in Metis private Zettelkasten:
- Raw voice memo transcriptions
- Half-formed thoughts
- Notes with confidence < 0.5
- Personal observations not yet synthesized

Implementation (when triggered):
1. Query Metis Zettelkasten for notes with confidence >= 0.7 and type=pattern
2. For each note:
   a. create_node in OHM (label=note title, content=note body, provenance='metis_zettelkasten')
   b. Find OHM nodes that match the note's [[wikilinks]]
   c. create_edge for each wikilink (edge_type derived from link context)
3. Record projection in observation: value=count, notes="projected N patterns, M edges"
4. Return summary: nodes_created, edges_created, patterns_skipped

Configuration:
- OHM_ZETTELKASTEN_SYNC_THRESHOLD=500 (nodes)
- OHM_PROJECTION_INTERVAL=24h (cooldown between projections)

Prior work: OHM-kjb.1 was the original integration epic, closed as the architecture evolved.

Estimated: ~120 lines (Python script + CLI command)
acceptance_criteria:
1. When triggered, pattern notes from Zettelkasten appear as OHM nodes
2. Wikilinks between notes create OHM edges
3. Metis notes below confidence threshold are not projected
4. Projection is idempotent (re-running doesn't duplicate nodes)
5. OHM observations table records projection metadata

title: BUG: /register validates display name as identifier, rejects Unicode
description:
/register validates the 'name' field as a SQL identifier (alphanumeric, underscores, hyphens, dots only). This rejects Unicode display names like 'Métis'. The display name is a human-readable label, not an identifier — the agent identifier comes from the auth token.

Fix: Skip identifier validation for the 'name' field in /register. The agent_id is constructed from the sanitized token-derived agent name, not the display name.

Fixed in commit e7e251c.
acceptance_criteria:
1. /register accepts Unicode display names (e.g., 'Métis')
2. Agent ID is still constructed from safe token-derived identifier
3. No SQL injection risk from display names (they're parameterized, not interpolated
close_reason: Fixed: /register no longer validates display name as identifier. Commit e7e251c.

title: BUG: SDK OHMClient allows unauthenticated reads (no token required)
description:
The SDK OHMClient (ohm.client.OHMClient) works without a token for read operations like stats(). The server accepts unauthenticated reads.

Testing:
- SDKClient(actor='metis') without token → stats() returns data
- SDKClient(actor='metis', token='...') with token → stats() returns data

This may be intentional (public read, authenticated write) but should be documented. If reads should require auth, the SDK should not silently succeed without a token.

Impact: Security concern if read access should be restricted. If intentional, document that reads are public and writes require auth.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Document auth policy: public read / authenticated write OR full auth required
2. SDK client raises clear error if token missing and auth is required
3. If public read is intentional, SDK works without token (current behavior)
close_reason: Security question: is public-read intentional? Filing as decision bead.

title: BUG: /node/find_or_create returns 201 even when finding existing node (should be 200)
description:
POST /node/find_or_create correctly returns the existing node (content='original' is preserved), but returns HTTP 201 instead of 200. 

REST convention: 201 means 'created a new resource'. When find_or_create finds an existing node, it should return 200 (found existing) not 201 (created new). This allows callers to distinguish between 'I found something' vs 'I created something new'.

Current: find_or_create always returns 201 regardless of whether node was found or created.
Expected: find_or_create returns 200 if found existing, 201 if created new.
acceptance_criteria:
1. find_or_create returns 200 when finding existing node
2. find_or_create returns 201 when creating new node
3. Callers can distinguish find vs create by HTTP status code
close_reason: Fixed: find_or_create returns 200 existing, 201 new. Commit e7e251c.

title: BUG: /path endpoint returns empty array for connected nodes
description:
The GET /path/{from}/{to} endpoint returns an empty array [] even when a path exists between the two nodes.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Node A has APPLIES_TO edge to node B
2. GET /path/A/B
3. Returns: []

The edge exists (verified via /neighborhood), but /path doesn't find it. This may be a directionality issue (path following only follows outgoing edges, or only follows specific edge types) or a query bug.

Impact: Agents can't use /path to find traversal routes between concepts in the graph.
acceptance_criteria:
1. /path/{from}/{to} returns the shortest path when one exists
2. Path follows edges regardless of direction or type
3. Returns the sequence of nodes and edges in the path
close_reason: Fixed: /path follows edges bidirectionally with cycle detection. Verified: found APPLIES_TO path from authority_escalation to AND→OR.

title: CLEANUP: Orphan agent nodes from pre-idempotent registration (metis_8b678b, metis_b0a2d2)
description:
Before OHM-5n7 (idempotent registration), agents could register multiple times, creating duplicate nodes. Two orphan Metis agent nodes exist:
- metis_8b678b: created by atlas
- metis_b0a2d2: created by atlas

These cannot be deleted by metis (403 ownership guard) because they were created by atlas. They should be cleaned up by an admin or the creating agent.

Additionally, the ownership guard on DELETE prevents any agent from cleaning up nodes they didn't create, even for stale test nodes. This means orphan cleanup requires the original creator's token or an admin endpoint.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Orphan agent nodes cleaned up (metis_8b678b, metis_b0a2d2 deleted)
2. Consider adding admin DELETE endpoint or bulk cleanup command
3. Consider relaxing ownership guard for agent-type nodes that match an existing deterministic ID

title: FEATURE: Metis zettelkasten→OHM bridge — project patterns into shared graph
description:
Métis's zettelkasten has pattern notes and discovery logic that should be projected into OHM where other agents can find, challenge, and extend them.

Current state: Métis manually created 4 pattern nodes in OHM (AND→OR conversion, autocatalytic text system, evaluation trap, external cognition) with edges to existing value/pattern nodes.

Desired state: An automated bridge that:
1. Reads pattern/synthesis notes from the zettelkasten
2. Projects them into OHM as pattern/concept nodes with L3 APPLIES_TO edges
3. Projects source notes as L2 REFERENCES edges
4. Records observations (confidence, sigma) for pattern strength
5. Runs periodically during heartbeat or on zettelkasten sync

The bridge should be one-directional (zettelkasten → OHM) — OHM is for shared awareness, not replacing local memory. Challenges from other agents flow back via /listen().

Architecture: A script at /root/olympus/metis/graph/ohm_bridge.py that:
- Reads from graph/zettelkasten.kuzu
- Uses ohm_client.OHMClient to write to OHM
- Tracks projected notes in a local mapping file to avoid duplicates
- Runs as part of heartbeat or on-demand

Activation: When OHM has 500+ nodes or agents report search relevance issues.
close_reason: Deferred to 500+ nodes, but improving description.

title: RESEARCH: Semantic search via embeddings and VSS index
description:
OHM currently uses ILIKE for text search, which works at <1K nodes but won't scale semantically. DuckDB's VSS extension supports HNSW indexes and cosine similarity search — both tested and working.

The bigger question: should OHM generate embeddings, or should agents provide them?

Option A — Agent-side embedding:
- Agents generate embeddings via Ollama (nomic-embed-text, 768-dim) when creating nodes
- POST /node accepts optional 'embedding' field (FLOAT[] or base64)
- OHM stores it and indexes with HNSW
- GET /search?mode=semantic&q_vector=... or POST /search with content that gets embedded server-side

Option B — Server-side embedding:
- OHM calls an embedding model on node creation/update
- Requires embedding service (Ollama API endpoint in config)
- More automatic but adds a dependency and latency

Option C — Hybrid:
- Agents CAN provide embeddings, but OHM also has an optional embedding service
- If no embedding provided and service configured, generate one
- Best flexibility, most complexity

Métris's zettelkasten already uses embedding-based similarity for discovery (discover.py suggest). The convergence point: OHM becomes the shared graph where embedding-based semantic search complements hop-based neighborhood traversal.

Activation trigger: When OHM hits 500+ nodes or agents report search relevance issues.

DuckDB VSS extension verified working:
- HNSW index creation: CREATE INDEX USING HNSW (embedding) WITH (metric = 'cosine')
- Cosine similarity: array_cosine_similarity(embedding, query_vector)
- Tested on DuckDB 1.5.2

FTS extension note: DuckDB FTS v1.5.2 has a PRAGMA syntax issue (treats index name as column ref). ILIKE is sufficient at current scale. Revisit FTS when search performance degrades or when DuckDB fixes the syntax.

title: FEATURE: Label substring filtering on list_nodes endpoint
description:
Current behavior: GET /nodes supports filtering by node_type and exact label match. No substring/pattern matching on labels.

Use case: Deepthought wants list_nodes(node_type='concept', label_contains='china') to find all concept nodes with 'china' in the label. Currently must use /search which only does text matching on a single query term.

Proposed API extension:
  GET /nodes?type=concept&label_contains=china&limit=20&offset=0
  
  Also support label_prefix:
  GET /nodes?label_prefix=hormuz → matches 'Hormuz Ceasefire', 'Hormuz Analysis', etc.

Implementation notes:
- DuckDB LIKE '%china%' for substring, LIKE 'hormuz%' for prefix
- Case-insensitive matching (ILIKE)
- Add indexes if needed for performance at scale
- Can be combined with existing type filter

Verified need: Deepthought integration testing 2026-05-18.
acceptance_criteria:
1. GET /nodes?label_contains=<term> returns nodes with matching labels (case-insensitive)
2. GET /nodes?label_prefix=<term> returns nodes with matching label prefix
3. Works with existing type, limit, offset parameters
4. Performance: <50ms on 1000+ node graph

title: ohmd: SSE /events filter by layer and agent
description: Currently /events streams all changes. An agent that only cares about L3 changes from Vegetation Agent must receive the entire feed and filter client-side. Add query params to /events: ?layer=L3,L4&agent=vegetation_agent&node_type=PASTURE. This reduces noise for agents that don't need full coverage. Implementation: the SSE subscriber registry already exists (from OH-M-9hx.2); just add filtering logic before pushing events to each subscriber.
acceptance_criteria: GET /events?layer=L3&agent=vegetation_agent returns only L3 events from vegetation agent; multiple filters combinable (?layer=L1,L2&node_type=ANIMAL); no auth required for filter params; backward compatible (no filter = full feed)
